title.zh: 卷六十四 吳延史盧趙列傳
title.en: Volume 64: Biographies of Wu, Yan, Shi, Lu, Zhao
title.raw: 卷六十四 吳延史盧趙列傳 Volume 64: Biographies of Wu, Yan, Shi, Lu, Zhao

sentences.zh: 吳佑字季英，[一]陳留長垣人也。

translations.literal: Wu You, courtesy name Jiying, was a man of Changyuan in Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Wu You, whose courtesy name was Jiying, came from Changyuan in Chenliu commandery.

sentences.zh: 父恢，為南海太守。

translations.literal: His father Hui served as Administrator of Nanhai.
translations.idiomatic: His father Wu Hui was the Administrator of Nanhai.

sentences.zh: [二]佑年十二，隨從到官。

translations.literal: When You was twelve he followed his father to his post.
translations.idiomatic: At the age of twelve he accompanied his father to the yamen.

sentences.zh: 恢欲殺青簡以寫經書，[三]佑諫曰：「今大人踰越五領，[四]遠在海濱，其俗誠陋，然舊多珍怪，上為國家所疑，下為權戚所望。

translations.literal: Hui wished to “kill the green” on bamboo slips to copy the classics; You remonstrated, saying: “Now my lord has crossed the Five Ridges and dwells far on the coast; the customs are indeed crude, yet of old there were many rare curiosities—above, the state suspects such things; below, powerful relatives look to them for gifts.
translations.idiomatic: When his father planned to cure bamboo slips for copying the classics, Wu You urged him not to: “You have crossed the Five Ridges and sit far down the coast. The folkways are rough, but this region has long been known for rare treasures. That draws suspicion from the court above and greedy expectation from the great families below.”

sentences.zh: [五]此書若成，則載之兼兩。

translations.literal: If this book is completed, it will fill more than two cartloads.
translations.idiomatic: If that book is finished, it will take two full cartloads to carry.

sentences.zh: [六]昔馬援以薏苡興謗，王陽以衣囊徼名。

translations.literal: In former times Ma Yuan raised slander with coix seed, Wang Yang sought a name with his traveling bags.
translations.idiomatic: Think of Ma Yuan, whose bags of coix seeds started a rumor of graft, or Wang Yang, who won a reputation with nothing but his luggage.

sentences.zh: [七]嫌疑之閒，誠先賢所慎也。

translations.literal: In the space between suspicion and blame, this is truly what the worthies of old were cautious about.
translations.idiomatic: The narrow ground between suspicion and scandal is exactly where past sages trod most carefully.

sentences.zh: 」恢乃止，撫其首曰：「吳氏世不乏季子矣。

translations.literal: ” Hui then desisted, stroked his head and said: “The house of Wu has never lacked a Jizi.”
translations.idiomatic: Wu Hui gave up the plan, stroked his son’s head, and said, “Our line has never wanted for a Jizi.”

sentences.zh: 」[八]及年二十，喪父，居無簷石，而不受贍遺。

translations.literal: When he reached twenty he lost his father; he had not a stone’s weight of grain under his eaves, yet he would not accept gifts of support.
translations.idiomatic: At twenty he buried his father. He had no store of grain at home, yet he refused charity from others.

sentences.zh: 常牧豕於長垣澤中，[九]行吟經書。

translations.literal: He often herded pigs in the marshes of Changyuan, walking and chanting the classics.
translations.idiomatic: He tended pigs in the Changyuan wetlands, pacing and reciting the classics aloud.

sentences.zh: 遇父故人，謂曰：「卿二千石子而自業賤事，縱子無恥，柰先君何？

translations.literal: He met an old friend of his father, who said to him: “You are the son of a two-thousand-dan official, yet you pursue a mean occupation yourself; even if you feel no shame, what of your late father?”
translations.idiomatic: An old acquaintance of his father found him there and said, “You are a governor’s son, yet you debased yourself with menial work. Suppose you feel no shame—what would your late father say?”

sentences.zh: 」佑辭謝而已，守志如初。

translations.literal: You declined with thanks only, and kept his resolve as at first.
translations.idiomatic: Wu You only bowed and excused himself, then went on living as he had chosen.

sentences.zh: 注[一]佑音又。

translations.literal: Note [1]: You is read “you” (same as 又).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The character 佑 is glossed with the homophone 又.

sentences.zh: 續漢書作「佑」。

translations.literal: The Continued Book of Han writes the name with the same graph as in the headnote gloss.
translations.idiomatic: The Continuation of the Han spells his personal name with the character noted in commentary [1].

sentences.zh: 注[二]「恢」或作「惔」，音徒濫反。

translations.literal: Note [2]: “Hui” is sometimes written 惔, read du lan fan (entering tone).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The father’s name appears as 惔 in some texts, with the fanqie reading 徒濫反.

sentences.zh: 注[三]殺青者，以火炙簡令汗，取其青易書，復不蠹，謂之殺青，亦謂汗簡。

translations.literal: Note [3]: “Killing the green” means to roast the slip with fire so that the sap sweats out, taking the green so it is easy to write on and will not be worm-eaten; this is called killing the green, also called sweating the slip.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Killing the green” means drying bamboo slips over a flame so the surface sweats, takes ink, and resists worms—also called “sweating the slips.”

sentences.zh: 義見劉向別錄也。

translations.literal: The meaning is seen in Liu Xiang’s Bie lu as well.
translations.idiomatic: The same explanation appears in Liu Xiang’s separate catalogue of works.

sentences.zh: 注[四]領者，西自衡山之南，東至於海，一山之限耳，別標名則有五焉。

translations.literal: Note [4]: A “ridge” (ling) means, west from south of Mount Heng to the sea, the limit of one mountain range; when named separately there are five.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Here “ridge” means the barrier formed by the Nanling range from south of Mount Heng eastward to the sea; five passes along it bear separate names.

sentences.zh: 裴氏廣*

translations.literal: Pei’s Guang*
translations.idiomatic: Pei’s Guang—

translations.literal: (Chuan)
translations.idiomatic: (Chuan)

sentences.zh: **[州]*記云：「大庾、始安、臨賀、桂陽、揭陽，是為五領。

translations.literal: **[Zhou]* Record says: “Dayu, Shi’an, Linhe, Guiyang, and Jieyang—these are the Five Ridges.”
translations.idiomatic: The Guangzhou ji states: “The Five Ridges are Dayu, Shi’an, Linhe, Guiyang, and Jieyang.”

sentences.zh: 」鄧德明南康記曰：「大庾，一也；

translations.literal: ” Deng Deming’s Nankang ji says: “Dayu is one;
translations.idiomatic: Deng Deming’s Record of Nankang says, “First, Dayu;

sentences.zh: 桂陽甲騎，二也；

translations.literal: the armored cavalry of Guiyang is two;
translations.idiomatic: second, the armored horsemen of Guiyang;

sentences.zh: 九真都龐，三也；

translations.literal: Dulang of Jiuzhen is three;
translations.idiomatic: third, Dulang in Jiuzhen;

sentences.zh: 臨賀萌渚，四也；

translations.literal: Mengzhu of Linhe is four;
translations.idiomatic: fourth, Mengzhu in Linhe;

sentences.zh: 始安越城，五也。

translations.literal: Yuecheng of Shi’an is five.”
translations.idiomatic: fifth, Yuecheng in Shi’an.”

sentences.zh: 」裴氏之說則為審矣。

translations.literal: ” Pei’s account is thus the more reliable.
translations.idiomatic: Pei Songzhi’s version is the sounder identification.

sentences.zh: 注[五]希望其贈遺也。

translations.literal: Note [5]: They hope for his gifts.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: That is, people hope he will send them presents.

sentences.zh: 注[六]車有兩輪，故稱「兩」也。

translations.literal: Note [6]: A cart has two wheels, hence it is called “a pair” (liang).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: A cart has two wheels, so a double load is called “two pairs” (jian liang).

sentences.zh: 注[七]徼，要也，音工堯反。

translations.literal: Note [7]: Jiao means “to seek,” read gong yao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: 徼 means “to angle for”; the fanqie is 工堯反.

sentences.zh: 前書曰，王陽好車馬，衣服鮮明，而遷徙轉移，所載不過囊橐。

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: Wang Yang loved carriages and horses; his clothes were bright, yet in moving from place to place he carried no more than bags and satchels.
translations.idiomatic: The Hanshu notes that Wang Yang loved fine horses and bright dress, yet when he moved house he took only satchels and sacks.

sentences.zh: 時人怪其奢，伏其儉，故俗傳王陽能作黃金。

translations.literal: People of the time marveled at his extravagance yet admired his frugality, so vulgar tradition spread that Wang Yang could make gold.
translations.idiomatic: Onlookers thought him extravagant in appearance but thrifty in fact, and gossip soon claimed he could transmute gold.

sentences.zh: 注[八]季子謂季札也。

translations.literal: Note [8]: “Jizi” refers to Jizha.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Jizi” means Prince Jizha of Wu.

sentences.zh: 注[九]續漢書曰「年四十餘，乃為郡吏」也。

translations.literal: Note [9]: The Continued Book of Han says “only after forty-some years did he become a clerk in the commandery.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Xù Hànshū adds that he did not take a county post until he was past forty.

sentences.zh: 後舉孝廉，[一]將行，郡中為祖道，佑越□共小史雍丘黃真歡語移時，與結友而別。

translations.literal: Later he was recommended as Filial and Incorrupt; when he was about to depart, the commandery held the farewell sacrifice on the road for him; You left his place at the feast and spoke at length in friendly talk with a petty clerk, Huang Zhen of Yongqiu, and they parted having made friends.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as Filial and Incorrupt, he was about to leave when the whole commandery gathered for the road-side blessing. He rose from the gathering and spent a long while in easy talk with a minor clerk, Huang Zhen of Yongqiu, and they parted as friends.

sentences.zh: [二]功曹以佑倨，請黜之。

translations.literal: The merit assessor, considering You arrogant, asked that he be dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: The merit clerk thought this arrogant and asked the governor to strike Wu You from the list.

sentences.zh: 太守曰：「吳季英有知人之明，卿且勿言。」

translations.literal: The Administrator said: “Wu Jiying has an eye for men; for the moment, say nothing.”
translations.idiomatic: The governor replied, “Wu Jiying knows how to read people. Say no more for now.”

sentences.zh: 真後亦舉孝廉，除新蔡長，世稱其清節。

translations.literal: Later Zhen too was recommended as Filial and Incorrupt, appointed magistrate of Xincai, and his generation praised his purity of conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Zhen was later recommended as Filial and Incorrupt, became magistrate of Xincai, and won fame for incorruptibility.

sentences.zh: [三]時公沙穆來游太學，無資糧，乃變服客傭，為佑賃舂。

translations.literal: At that time Gongsha Mu came to study at the Imperial Academy; having no funds for provisions, he changed his clothes and hired himself out as a guest laborer, renting himself to pound grain for You.
translations.idiomatic: When Gongsha Mu arrived at the Imperial Academy without money for food, he disguised himself and hired out as a laborer, pounding grain in Wu You’s household.

sentences.zh: 佑與語大驚，遂共定交於杵臼之閒。

translations.literal: You conversed with him and was greatly startled, and so they fixed friendship between mortar and pestle.
translations.idiomatic: A single conversation astonished Wu You, and the two became friends there at the mill.

sentences.zh: 注[一]陳留耆舊傳曰：「太守冷宏召補文學，宏見異之，擢舉孝廉。」

translations.literal: Note [1]: The Chenliu qijiu zhuan says: “Administrator Leng Hong summoned him to fill a literary post; Hong saw he was extraordinary and promoted him as Filial and Incorrupt.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Chenliu qijiu zhuan records that Governor Leng Hong first appointed him to a literary post, then singled him out and nominated him as Filial and Incorrupt.

sentences.zh: 注[二]祖道之禮，封土為軷□也。

translations.literal: Note [2]: The rite of blessing the road is to heap earth for the ba mound.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The farewell sacrifice on the road involved heaping earth for the ba (threshold) rite.

sentences.zh: 五經要義曰：「祖道者，行祭為道路祈也。

translations.literal: The Essentials of the Five Classics says: “Those who bless the road perform a sacrifice to pray for the journey.”
translations.idiomatic: The Wujing yaoyi explains it as a sacrifice to pray for a safe journey.

sentences.zh: 」周禮太馭：「掌王玉路以祀，及*

translations.literal: ” Rites of Zhou, Grand Charioteer: “He holds the king’s jade chariot for sacrifice, and for*
translations.idiomatic: The Zhou li, office of the Grand Charioteer, says he drives the king’s jade chariot for sacrifice and for—

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si)

translations.literal: **[fan]* the ba.”
translations.idiomatic: —crossing the ba mound.”

translations.literal: ” The commentary says: “*[Fan]* the ba*
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan’s commentary glosses “crossing the ba” as—

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si)

sentences.zh: *者，封土象山於路側，以*[菩]*芻□□為神主祭之，以車轢軷而去。

translations.literal: * means to heap earth like a mountain at the side of the road, take *[pu]* straw and reeds as the spirit host and sacrifice to it, then roll the chariot over the ba and depart.
translations.idiomatic: —heaping earth beside the road like a small hill, setting a spirit seat of straw, offering sacrifice, then driving the wheels across it to symbolize an unhindered road.

sentences.zh: 喻無險難。」

translations.literal: It is a metaphor for having no peril or hardship.”
translations.idiomatic: The image means “no peril or hardship ahead.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰：「真字夏甫。」

translations.literal: Note [3]: Xie Cheng’s book says: “Zhen’s courtesy name was Xiafu.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Xie Cheng’s Hou Hànshū gives Huang Zhen’s courtesy name as Xiafu.

sentences.zh: 佑以光祿四行遷膠東侯相。

translations.literal: You by the four categories of the Director of Retainers was transferred to be marquis-administrator of Jiaodong.
translations.idiomatic: On the strength of the Director of Retainers’ “four virtues” nomination he was promoted to Marquis-Administrator of Jiaodong.

sentences.zh: [一]時濟北戴宏父為縣丞，宏年十六，從在丞捨。

translations.literal: At that time Dai Hong of Jibei, whose father was assistant magistrate of the county, was sixteen and lived with his father in the assistant’s quarters.
translations.idiomatic: At that time Dai Hong of Jibei was sixteen; his father was a county assistant, and the boy lived with him in the yamen quarters.

sentences.zh: 佑每行園，常聞諷誦之音，奇而厚之，亦與為友，卒成儒宗，知名東夏，[二] 官至酒泉太守。

translations.literal: Whenever You walked the garden he always heard the sound of recitation; he marveled at it and treated him generously, and also befriended him; he finally became a patriarch of Confucian learning, known in the eastern summer, and rose to the rank of Administrator of Jiuquan.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Wu You strolled the grounds he heard someone chanting texts; he sought the boy out, encouraged him, and befriended him. Dai Hong grew into a leading scholar of the east and eventually reached the post of Administrator of Jiuquan.

sentences.zh: [三]佑政唯仁簡，以身率物。

translations.literal: Note [3]: You’s government was only benevolent and simple; he led others by his own person.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Wu You governed with kindness and restraint, setting the example himself.

sentences.zh: 民有爭訴者，輒閉合自責，然後斷其訟，以道譬之。

translations.literal: When the people had quarrels and lawsuits he would shut his doors and blame himself, then decide their suit, instructing them with the Way.
translations.idiomatic: If commoners came to court with a quarrel, he first shut himself in to reflect on his own faults, then opened the case and reasoned with them in moral terms.

sentences.zh: 或身到閭裡，重相和解。

translations.literal: Sometimes he went in person to the lanes and hamlets to urge reconciliation again.
translations.idiomatic: Sometimes he walked into the neighborhoods to patch up feuds in person.

sentences.zh: 自是之後，爭隙省息，吏人懷而不欺。

translations.literal: After this, quarrels and ill will grew rare; clerks and people cherished him and did not deceive him.
translations.idiomatic: After that, lawsuits and grudges dwindled; officials and townsfolk trusted him and ceased deceit.

sentences.zh: 嗇夫孫性私賦民錢，[四]市衣以進其父，父得而怒曰：「有君如是，何忍欺之！

translations.literal: The bailiff Sun Xing privately assessed the people for money, bought clothes to present to his father; his father received them and angrily said: “With a lord like this, how could you bear to cheat him!
translations.idiomatic: A bailiff named Sun Xing secretly taxed the villagers to buy his father a robe. When the old man saw it he grew angry: “We have such a magistrate—how dare you cheat him!”

sentences.zh: 」促歸伏罪。

translations.literal: ” He urged him to return and confess his crime.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered his son to go back and confess.

sentences.zh: 性籩懼，詣合持衣自首。

translations.literal: Xing was frightened in his panic, went to the gate holding the clothes and surrendered himself.
translations.idiomatic: Terrified, Sun Xing took the garment to the yamen gate and gave himself up.

sentences.zh: 佑屏左右問其故，性具談父言。

translations.literal: You sent away those at his left and right and asked the reason; Xing told in full his father’s words.
translations.idiomatic: Wu You dismissed his attendants and asked what had happened; Sun Xing repeated his father’s rebuke word for word.

sentences.zh: 佑曰：「掾以親故，受□穢之名，所謂『觀過斯知人矣』。

translations.literal: You said: “As clerk, for a parent’s sake you have taken a tainted name—this is what is called ‘see his faults and you know the man.’
translations.idiomatic: Wu You said, “You stained your office for a father’s sake—that is what the Analects mean by ‘study his faults and you know his character.’

sentences.zh: 」[五]使歸謝其父，還以衣遺之。

translations.literal: ” He sent him home to thank his father, and returned the clothes to give to him.
translations.idiomatic: He sent him home to thank his father and let him keep the clothes.

sentences.zh: 又安丘男子毋丘長與母俱行市，道遇醉客辱其母，長殺之而亡，安丘追蹤於膠東得之。

translations.literal: Also a man of Anqiu, Wuqiu Chang, was walking in the market with his mother when they met a drunk who insulted his mother; Chang killed him and fled, and Anqiu tracked him to Jiaodong and seized him.
translations.idiomatic: A man of Anqiu, Wuqiu Chang, was in the market with his mother when a drunk humiliated her; Chang killed the man and fled. Anqiu officers traced him to Jiaodong and arrested him.

sentences.zh: 佑呼長謂曰：「子母見辱，人情所恥。

translations.literal: You called Chang and said to him: “For a son to see his mother insulted is a thing human feeling finds shameful.
translations.idiomatic: Wu You had him brought in and said, “Any son would burn with shame when his mother is insulted.

sentences.zh: 然孝子忿必慮難，動不累親。

translations.literal: Yet a filial son’s anger must consider difficulty; in acting he does not drag down his kin.
translations.idiomatic: Yet a dutiful son weighs the danger before he strikes, so his kin are not ruined with him.

sentences.zh: [六]今若背親逞怒，[七]白日殺人，赦若非義，刑若不忍，將如之何？

translations.literal: Note [6] Now if you turn your back on kin to indulge your rage, Note [7] and kill a man in broad daylight—if to pardon would offend justice, if to execute would offend compassion—what then?
translations.idiomatic: But you cast off your duty to your mother to indulge your wrath and killed in open day. To spare you would betray the law; to behead you would seem heartless. What should I do?”

sentences.zh: 」長以械自系，[八]曰：「國家製法，囚身犯之。

translations.literal: ” Chang put the fetters on himself, Note [8] saying: “The state has set the laws; I, the prisoner, have broken them.
translations.idiomatic: Wuqiu Chang locked the stocks on himself and said, “The statutes are fixed—I am the criminal who broke them.

sentences.zh: 明府雖加哀鄉，恩無所施。」

translations.literal: Though my lord would show pity, there is nowhere to apply favor.”
translations.idiomatic: Even your mercy cannot undo what the code demands.”

sentences.zh: 佑問長有妻子乎？

translations.literal: You asked whether Chang had a wife.
translations.idiomatic: Wu You asked whether he had a wife.

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有妻未有子也。

translations.literal: He answered: “I have a wife but no child yet.
translations.idiomatic: He said he was married but had no son yet.

sentences.zh: 」即移安丘逮長妻，妻到，解其桎梏，使同宿獄中，妻遂懷孕。

translations.literal: ” He at once sent word to Anqiu to bring Chang’s wife; when the wife arrived he released his fetters and let them spend the night together in the prison, and the wife then became pregnant.
translations.idiomatic: Wu You sent to Anqiu for the wife, removed his shackles when she arrived, and left them together in the cell until she conceived.

sentences.zh: 至冬盡行刑，長泣謂母曰：「負母應死，當何以報吳君乎？

translations.literal: When winter ended and the execution was carried out, Chang wept and said to his mother: “I have failed my mother and deserve death—how shall I repay Lord Wu?
translations.idiomatic: When winter closed and the sentence was carried out, he wept to his mother, “I wronged you and must die—how can I ever repay Magistrate Wu?”

sentences.zh: 」乃嚙指而吞之，含血言曰：「妻若生子，名之『吳生』，言我臨死吞指為誓，屬兒以報吳君。

translations.literal: ” He then bit off his finger and swallowed it, and with blood in his mouth said: “If my wife bears a child, name him ‘Born-of-Wu,’ meaning that at the point of death I swallowed my finger in oath, charging the child to repay Lord Wu.
translations.idiomatic: He bit off a finger, swallowed it, and with blood on his lips said, “If my wife bears a son, name him ‘Wu’s Child,’ so he will know I swore with my lifeblood to repay the magistrate.”

sentences.zh: 」因投繯而死。

translations.literal: ” Then he cast himself into the noose and died.
translations.idiomatic: Then he slipped the noose around his neck and hanged himself.

sentences.zh: [九] 注[一]漢官儀曰「四行，敦厚、質樸、遜讓、節儉」也。

translations.literal: Note [9] Note [1]: The Han guan yi says “the four categories are honest simplicity, plain substance, yielding modesty, and frugal restraint.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Han guan yi defines the Director of Retainers’ “four virtues” as honesty, plainness, modesty, and thrift.

sentences.zh: 注[二]東夏，東方也。

translations.literal: Note [2]: Eastern Summer means the eastern regions.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Eastern Summer” means the eastern quarter of the realm.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰「尹茲東夏」也。

translations.literal: The Documents says “thereby rule this eastern summer.”
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu speaks of “ruling this eastern summer” in the same sense.

sentences.zh: 注[三]濟北先賢傳曰「宏字符襄，剛縣人也。

translations.literal: Note [3]: The Jibei xianxian zhuan says “Hong’s courtesy name was Zixiang; he was a man of Gang county.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Jibei xianxian zhuan identifies Dai Hong, courtesy name Zixiang, a native of Gang county.

sentences.zh: 年二十二，為郡督郵，曾以職事見詰，府君欲撻之。

translations.literal: At twenty-two he was commandery dūyóu; once when questioned on official business the Administrator wished to flog him.
translations.idiomatic: At twenty-two he served as dūyóu; once the governor meant to flog him over a paperwork dispute.

sentences.zh: 宏曰：『今鄙郡遭明府，鹹以為仲尼之君，國小人少，以宏為顏回，豈聞仲尼有撻顏回之義？

translations.literal: Hong said: “Now my humble commandery has encountered my lord; all consider you a lord like Confucius; the state is small and the people few, and they take Hong for Yan Hui—have you heard of Confucius flogging Yan Hui?
translations.idiomatic: Dai Hong replied, “We call you another Confucius in this small commandery and think of me as Yan Hui—would Confucius beat Yan Hui?”

sentences.zh: 』府君異其對，即日教署主簿」也。

translations.literal: ” The Administrator marveled at his reply and that very day ordered him appointed chief clerk.”
translations.idiomatic: The governor admired the answer and appointed him chief clerk the same day.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]續漢書曰：「賦錢五百，為父市單衣。」

translations.literal: Note [4]: The Continued Book of Han says: “He assessed five hundred cash to buy his father a thin robe.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Xù Hànshū specifies that Sun Xing levied five hundred cash to buy his father a single-layer robe.

sentences.zh: 注[五]論語載孔子之言也。

translations.literal: Note [5]: It records a saying of Confucius in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The quotation is from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 注[六]論語孔子曰：「忿思難。

translations.literal: Note [6]: The Analects: Confucius says: “When angry, think of the difficulties.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Analects has Confucius say, “When moved to anger, consider the consequences.”

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「一朝之忿，忘其身以及其親，非惑與？」

translations.literal: ” It also says: “In a morning’s anger forgetting oneself and one’s kin—is that not folly?”
translations.idiomatic: It also says, “A fit of rage that costs you your life and shames your parents—is that not folly?”

sentences.zh: 注[七]若，汝也。

translations.literal: Note [7]: Ruo means “you.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: the graph glossed here is the second-person pronoun “you.”

sentences.zh: 逞，快也。

translations.literal: Cheng means “to gratify.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: the verb means to indulge a whim or gratify a passion.

sentences.zh: 注[八]在手曰械。

translations.literal: Note [8]: On the hands they are called fetters (xie).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Wooden cuffs on the hands are called xie.

sentences.zh: 注[九]謂以繩為繯，投之而縊也。

translations.literal: Note [9]: It means to use a cord as a noose and cast oneself to strangle.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: He fashioned a cord into a noose and strangled himself.

sentences.zh: 繯音胡犬反。

translations.literal: The graph huan (繯) is read hu quan fan.
translations.idiomatic: The character 繯 is glossed 胡犬反.

sentences.zh: 佑在膠東九年，[一]遷齊相，大將軍梁冀表為長史。

translations.literal: After nine years in Jiaodong, Note [1]: he was transferred to Administrator of Qi; Grand General Liang Ji memorialized him as senior clerk.
translations.idiomatic: After nine years in Jiaodong he became Administrator of Qi; Grand General Liang Ji then recommended him as senior clerk.

sentences.zh: 及冀誣奏太尉李固，佑聞而請見，與冀爭之，不聽。

translations.literal: When Ji slanderously memorialized against Grand Commandant Li Gu, You heard of it and asked an audience, arguing with Ji; Ji would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: When Liang Ji framed Grand Commandant Li Gu, Wu You demanded an audience and argued face to face; Liang Ji refused to listen.

sentences.zh: 時扶風馬融在坐，為冀章草，佑因謂融曰：「李公之罪，成於卿手。

translations.literal: At that time Ma Rong of Fufeng was seated with them, drafting the memorial for Ji; You therefore said to Rong: “Lord Li’s crime is being completed by your hand.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong of Fufeng sat nearby, drafting the memorial. Wu You told him, “You are finishing the work of destroying Li Gu.

sentences.zh: 李公即誅，卿何面目見天下之人乎？

translations.literal: When Lord Li is executed, with what face will you look upon the people of the realm?”
translations.idiomatic: When Li Gu dies, how will you meet anyone under heaven?”

sentences.zh: 」冀怒而起入室，佑亦徑去。

translations.literal: ” Ji rose in anger and went into an inner room; You also went straight away.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji stormed off to his private rooms; Wu You walked out without another word.

sentences.zh: 冀道出佑為河閒相，因自免歸家，不復仕，躬灌園蔬，以經書教授。

translations.literal: Ji had him sent out on the road as Administrator of Hejian; he therefore resigned on his own and returned home, serving no more, personally watering garden vegetables and teaching the classics.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji shunted him off as Administrator of Hejian; Wu You resigned at once, went home, never took office again, tended his garden, and taught the classics.

sentences.zh: 年九十八卒。

translations.literal: He died at ninety-eight.
translations.idiomatic: He died at the age of ninety-eight.

sentences.zh: 注[一]陳留耆舊傳曰：「佑處同僚，無私書之問，上司無箋檄之敬。

translations.literal: Note [1]: The Chenliu qijiu zhuan says: “Among colleagues You had no private correspondence; toward superiors he showed no fawning in memos.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Chenliu qijiu zhuan says he exchanged no private letters with colleagues and sent no obsequious paperwork to superiors.

sentences.zh: 在膠東，書不入京師也。」

translations.literal: While in Jiaodong, his correspondence did not enter the capital.”
translations.idiomatic: While he held Jiaodong he sent no private letters to anyone at court.”

sentences.zh: 長子鳳，官至樂浪太守，少子愷，新息令；

translations.literal: His eldest son Feng rose to the rank of Administrator of Lelang; his younger son Kai was magistrate of Xinxi;
translations.idiomatic: His eldest son rose to Administrator of Lelang; his younger son Kai became magistrate of Xinxi;

sentences.zh: 鳳子馮，鮦陽侯相：[一]皆有名於世。

translations.literal: Feng’s son Feng was marquis-administrator of Tongyang: Note [1] all were renowned in the world.
translations.idiomatic: Feng’s son served as Marquis-Administrator of Tongyang; every generation of the family earned a public name.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]鮦陽，縣，屬汝南郡。

translations.literal: Note [2] Note [1] Tongyang is a county under Runan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Tongyang county lay in Runan commandery.

sentences.zh: 音紂。

translations.literal: It is read like the character for “zhou” (the tyrant Zhou).
translations.idiomatic: The place name is pronounced like the name of the last Shang king.

sentences.zh: 注[二]陳留耆舊傳曰：「鳳字君雅，馮字子高。」

translations.literal: Note [2] The Chenliu qijiu zhuan says: “Feng’s courtesy name was Junya; Feng’s courtesy name was Zigao.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Chenliu qijiu zhuan names the elder son Feng with the courtesy name Junya and the grandson Feng with the courtesy name Zigao (different characters, same surname).

sentences.zh: 延篤字叔堅，南陽犨人也。

translations.literal: Yan Du, courtesy name Shujian, was a man of Chou in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du, whose courtesy name was Shujian, came from Chou in Nanyang commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]少從穎川唐溪典受左氏傳，[二]旬日能諷之，典深敬焉。

translations.literal: Note [1] In youth he studied the Zuo Tradition with Tang Xi Dian of Yingchuan; Note [2] within ten days he could recite it from memory, and Dian deeply respected him.
translations.idiomatic: As a boy he took the Zuo Tradition from Tang Xi Dian of Yingchuan and memorized it in a matter of days, to his teacher’s admiration.

sentences.zh: [三]又從馬融受業，博通經傳及百家之言，能著文章，有名京師。

translations.literal: Note [3] He also studied under Ma Rong, broadly mastering the classics, commentaries, and the writings of the hundred schools; he could compose essays and was famous in the capital.
translations.idiomatic: He went on to sit at Ma Rong’s feet, mastered the canon and masters’ glosses as well as miscellaneous learning, wrote well, and made a name in Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 注[一]犨音昌猶反，故城在汝州魯山縣東南也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Chou is read chang you fan; the old walled town lay southeast of Lushan county in Ruzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The place name is read chang-you in the fanqie system; the old walled town lay southeast of present-day Lushan in Henan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]先賢行狀曰：「典字季度，為西鄂長。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Xianxian xingzhuang says: “Dian’s courtesy name was Jidu; he was magistrate of Xi’e.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Lives of Worthies of Antiquity record Dian’s courtesy name as Jidu and his post as magistrate of Xi’e.

sentences.zh: 」風俗通曰：「吳夫□王奔楚，封堂溪，因以為氏。

translations.literal: ” The Fengsu tong says: “When the king of Wu fled to Chu he was enfeoffed at Tangxi, and the clan took that as its surname.
translations.idiomatic: The Fengsu tong traces the surname to a fugitive king of Wu enfeoffed at Tangxi in Chu.

sentences.zh: 」典為五官中郎將。

translations.literal: ” Dian became Colonel of the Five Offices.
translations.idiomatic: Tang Xi Dian later rose to Colonel of the Five Offices.

sentences.zh: 「唐」與「堂」同也。

translations.literal: “Tang” is the same as “Tang” (hall).
translations.idiomatic: The graphs for “Tang” and “hall” were interchangeable in this surname.

sentences.zh: 注[三]先賢行狀曰：「篤欲寫左氏傳，無紙，唐溪典以廢箋記與之。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Xianxian xingzhuang says: “Du wished to copy out the Zuo Tradition but had no paper; Tang Xi Dian gave him used note slips.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Yan Du lacked paper to copy the Zuo Tradition; Dian gave him scrap slips from old files.

sentences.zh: 篤以箋記紙不可寫傳，乃借本諷之，徹盡辭歸。

translations.literal: Du considered note-paper unsuited to copying the Tradition, so he borrowed the original, memorized it, and when he had gone through it all to the end he returned it and took his leave.
translations.idiomatic: He thought thin note paper wrong for a fair copy, borrowed the full scroll, learned it by heart, and handed it back when finished.

sentences.zh: 典曰：『卿欲寫傳，何故辭歸？

translations.literal: Dian said: “You wanted to copy the Tradition—why are you leaving?”
translations.idiomatic: Dian asked, “You meant to transcribe the text—why walk away now?”

sentences.zh: 』篤曰：『已諷之矣。

translations.literal: ” Du said: “I have already memorized it.”
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du answered, “It is already in my memory.”

sentences.zh: 』典聞之歎曰：『嗟乎延生！

translations.literal: ” Dian heard this and sighed: “Ah, Master Yan!
translations.idiomatic: Dian sighed, “Ah, young Yan!

sentences.zh: 雖復端木聞一知二，未足為喻。

translations.literal: Though Duanmu again heard one and knew two, it would not be enough for a comparison.
translations.idiomatic: Even Zigong, who grasped two corners from one hint, is no match for this.”

sentences.zh: 若使尼父更起於洙、泗，君當編名七十，與游、夏爭匹也。

translations.literal: If the Master of Ni should rise again between the Zhu and the Si, you ought to be numbered among the seventy disciples and vie with You and Bu.
translations.idiomatic: Had Confucius taught again by the Zhu and Si rivers, you would earn a place among the seventy and rank with You and Bu.

sentences.zh: 』」舉孝廉，為平陽侯相。

translations.literal: ”” He was recommended as Filial and Incorrupt and became marquis-administrator of Pingyang.
translations.idiomatic: ” He was nominated as Filial and Incorrupt and appointed Marquis-Administrator of Pingyang.

sentences.zh: 到官，表龔遂之墓，立銘祭祠，擢用其後於畎畝之閒。

translations.literal: When he arrived at his post he marked the tomb of Gong Sui, set up an inscription and offered sacrifice, and promoted Gong’s descendants from among the farmers.
translations.idiomatic: On taking office he restored Gong Sui’s neglected grave, inscribed a stele and sacrificed there, then raised Gong’s heirs from the fields to office.

sentences.zh: [一] 以師喪□官奔赴，五府並辟不就。

translations.literal: Note [1] Because of his teacher’s death he left his post and hurried to the funeral; the five high bureaus all summoned him but he would not go.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: He resigned to mourn a teacher; every top office tried to recruit him and he refused them all.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書龔遂，山陽南平陽人，為勃海太守。

translations.literal: Note [1] In the Former Documents, Gong Sui, a man of Nanpingyang in Shanyang, was Administrator of Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gong Sui of Nanpingyang in Shanyang had been Bohai’s governor under the Former Han.

sentences.zh: 南平陽故城*[在]*今兗州鄒縣。

translations.literal: The old town of Nanpingyang *[lies]* in Zou county of Yanzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: The Han site of Nanpingyang is within present-day Zou county in Shandong.

sentences.zh: 桓帝以博士征，拜議郎，與朱穆、邊韶共著作東觀。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan summoned him as an erudite, appointed him Court Gentleman, and set him to compose at the Eastern Pavilion with Zhu Mu and Bian Shao.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan called him up as a doctoral scholar, named him a Court Gentleman, and assigned him to compile history at the Eastern Pavilion beside Zhu Mu and Bian Shao.

sentences.zh: 稍遷侍中。

translations.literal: He was gradually promoted to palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: He rose in stages to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 帝數問政事，篤詭辭密對，[一]動依典義。

translations.literal: The emperor repeatedly questioned him on government; Du answered with subtle indirection, Note [1] always grounding himself in canonical principle.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign often pressed him on policy; Yan Du answered obliquely but always from the classics.

sentences.zh: 遷左馮翊，又徙京兆尹。

translations.literal: He was transferred to Administrator of Left Fengyi, then moved to Metropolitan Governor of the capital region.
translations.idiomatic: He became Governor of Left Fengyi, then Metropolitan Governor of Jingzhao.

sentences.zh: 其政用寬仁，憂恤民黎，擢用長者，與參政事，郡中歡愛，三輔咨嗟焉。

translations.literal: His administration relied on lenience and kindness; he pitied the common people, promoted the aged and worthy to share in government, and the commandery rejoiced in him; the three adjuncts sighed his name with praise.
translations.idiomatic: He governed with mercy, cared for the poor, brought gray-haired local leaders into counsel, and won such love in the capital region that the whole triple belt spoke his praise.

sentences.zh: 先是陳留邊鳳為京兆尹，亦有能名，郡人為之語曰：「前有趙張三王，[二]後有邊延二君。

translations.literal: Earlier Bian Feng of Chenliu had been Metropolitan Governor and also had a reputation for ability; the people of the commandery made a saying of it: “Before there were Zhao, Zhang, and the three Wangs, Note [2] afterward came the two lords Bian and Yan.”
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Bian Feng of Chenliu had held the same post with equal talent, so locals coined a rhyme: “First Zhao, Zhang, and the three Wangs; then Bian and Yan, two sterling lords.”

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]谷梁傳曰：「故士造辟而言，詭辭而出。

translations.literal: ” Note [1] The Guliang zhuan says: “Therefore the scholar, on entering audience, speaks plainly, and with indirection withdraws.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Guliang commentary contrasts straight speech in court with oblique withdrawal.

sentences.zh: 」范寧注云：「辟，君也。

translations.literal: ” Fan Ning’s commentary says: “Bi means the lord.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Ning glosses bi as “the ruler.”

sentences.zh: 詭辭而出，不以實告人也。」

translations.literal: With indirection one withdraws—one does not report the truth to others.”
translations.idiomatic: “Indirection” means leaving without laying the whole truth bare.

sentences.zh: 注[二]前書，趙廣漢、張敞、王遵、王章、王駿俱為京兆尹也。

translations.literal: Note [2] In the Former Documents, Zhao Guanghan, Zhang Chang, Wang Zun, Wang Zhang, and Wang Jun were all Metropolitan Governors.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The “three Wangs” with Zhao and Zhang are the famous Former Han governors of the capital.

sentences.zh: 時皇子有疾，下郡縣出珍藥，而大將軍梁冀遣客繼書詣京兆，並貨牛黃。

translations.literal: At that time an imperial son was ill, and an edict went to commanderies and counties to furnish rare drugs; Grand General Liang Ji sent agents with letters in succession to Jingzhao, all seeking to buy ox bezoar.
translations.idiomatic: When a prince fell sick, counties were ordered to send rare medicines; Liang Ji’s clients flooded Jingzhao with orders to corner the market in bezoar.

sentences.zh: [一] 篤發書收客，曰：「大將軍椒房外家，而皇子有疾，必應陳進醫方，豈當使客千里求利乎？

translations.literal: Note [1] Du opened the letters, seized the agents, and said: “The Grand General is kin by marriage to the palace; when a prince is sick he ought to submit prescriptions, not send clients a thousand li to turn a profit!
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du broke the seals, arrested the messengers, and said, “The general is imperial in-law; if the boy is ill he should offer cures, not run a long-distance trade in bezoar!”

sentences.zh: 」遂殺之。

translations.literal: ” He then executed them.
translations.idiomatic: He had them put to death.

sentences.zh: 冀籩而不得言，有司承旨欲求其事。

translations.literal: Ji was furious but could not speak of it; responsible officials, reading his mood, tried to find grounds against Du.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji fumed in silence; his creatures hunted for a charge against the governor.

sentences.zh: 篤以病免歸，教授家巷。

translations.literal: Du pleaded illness, resigned, and returned home to teach in his lane.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du claimed illness, went home, and taught from his house.

sentences.zh: 注[一]吳普本草曰：「牛黃味苦，無毒，牛出入呻者有之。

translations.literal: Note [1] Wu Pu’s Materia medica says: “Ox bezoar is bitter and non-toxic; it forms in cattle that low when moving in and out.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Wu Pu describes bezoar as bitter, harmless, and found in cattle that low strangely.

sentences.zh: 夜有光走角中。

translations.literal: At night there is light running in the horns.
translations.idiomatic: At night a glow sometimes runs along the horns.

sentences.zh: 牛死，入膽中，如□子黃。

translations.literal: When the ox dies it enters the gall bladder, like the yellow of a □ seed.
translations.idiomatic: After death it congeals in the gall like a ball of yolk.

sentences.zh: 」神農本草曰：「療驚□，除邪逐鬼。」

translations.literal: ” The Shennong materia medica says: “It treats convulsions □ and drives out evil spirits.”
translations.idiomatic: The Shennong canon adds that it calms convulsions and expels malignant influences.

sentences.zh: 時人或疑仁孝前後之證，篤乃論之曰：「觀夫仁孝之辯，[一]紛然異端，互引典文，代取事據，[二]可謂篤論矣。

translations.literal: Some men of the time doubted the evidence for putting benevolence before filial piety or the reverse; Du therefore discussed it, saying: “Consider the dispute over benevolence and filial piety, Note [1] confusedly different schools cite canonical texts in turn and seize on precedents, Note [2]—one may call that a thorough argument.
translations.idiomatic: People were arguing whether benevolence or filial duty came first; Yan Du wrote, “The debate splinters into schools, each waving its own texts—yet all that citation only clouds the issue.

sentences.zh: [三]夫人二致同源，總率百行，[四]非復銖兩輕重，必定前後之數也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Now these two goals share one source, gather in the hundred conducts, Note [4] and are not again a matter of weighing ounces and drams to fix a definite order of before and after.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: In truth benevolence and filial piety spring from one root and inform every virtue; they are not beans on a scale that must be ranked first or second.

sentences.zh: 而如欲分其大較，[五]體而名之，則孝在事親，仁施品物。

translations.literal: Yet if you wish to divide their rough proportions, Note [5] embody them and name them, then filial piety lies in serving parents, benevolence in extending kindness to things.
translations.idiomatic: Still, if we sketch the difference: filial piety is love at home; benevolence is kindness spread abroad.

sentences.zh: 施物則功濟於時，事親則德歸於己。

translations.literal: Extending to things, merit aids the age; serving parents, virtue returns to oneself.
translations.idiomatic: Public kindness helps the world; honoring parents perfects the self.

sentences.zh: 於己則事寡，濟時則功多。

translations.literal: What returns to oneself involves few affairs; aiding the age involves much merit.
translations.idiomatic: The one is a narrow circle of duty; the other reaches far in time.

sentences.zh: 推此以言，仁則遠矣。

translations.literal: Pushing from this to speak of it, benevolience is the more far-reaching.
translations.idiomatic: On that reckoning benevolence looks the larger virtue.

sentences.zh: 然物有出微而著，事有由隱而章。

translations.literal: Yet things may go from minute to manifest, affairs from hidden to plain.
translations.idiomatic: Yet small roots can grow great, and hidden beginnings can end in glory.

sentences.zh: 近取諸身，刵耳有聽受之用，目有察見之明，足有致遠之勞，手有飾衛之功，功雖顯外，本之者心也。

translations.literal: Taking what is near from the person: the ears have the use of hearing, the eyes the brightness of seeing, the feet the labor of going far, the hands the work of adornment and defense—though the functions appear outwardly, what masters them is the heart.
translations.idiomatic: Close at hand, the ear hears, the eye sees, the foot travels, the hand defends—yet all these limbs obey the heart.

sentences.zh: 遠取諸物，則草木之生，始於萌牙，終於彌蔓，枝葉扶疏，榮華紛縟，[六]末雖繁蔚，致之者根也。

translations.literal: Taking what is far from things, then the birth of plants and trees begins in sprouting and ends in spreading riotously; branches and leaves grow thick, flowers and fruit run riot, Note [6] though the tips may flourish in profusion, what brings it about is the root.
translations.idiomatic: In nature, trees begin as shoots and end as forest; the crown may blaze with color, but the root commands the growth.

sentences.zh: 夫仁人之有孝，猶四體之有心腹，[七]枝葉之有本根也。

translations.literal: Now when a benevolent man possesses filial piety, it is as the four limbs have heart and belly, Note [7] as branches and leaves have root and trunk.
translations.idiomatic: So benevolence that includes filial piety is like a body with its trunk, or a tree with its living core.

sentences.zh: 聖人知之，故曰：

translations.literal: The sages knew this, and therefore said:
translations.idiomatic: The sages saw this and said:

sentences.zh: 『夫孝，天之經也，地之義也，人之行也。

translations.literal: “Filial piety is the warp of Heaven, the rightness of Earth, and the conduct of man.
translations.idiomatic: “Filial piety is Heaven’s pattern, Earth’s norm, and the way human beings should walk.”

sentences.zh: 』[八]『君子務本，本立而道生，孝悌也者，其為仁之本與！

translations.literal: ” Note [8] “The gentleman attends to the root; when the root is established, the Way grows—filial piety and fraternal duty are they not the root of benevolence?”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Analects adds that dutiful children at home are the soil from which benevolence grows.

sentences.zh: 』[九]然體大難備，物性好偏，故所施不同，事少兩兼者也。

translations.literal: ” Note [9] Yet the great body is hard to complete in every part; things and nature tend to partiality, hence what they extend differs, and few affairs combine both in full measure.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: No one embodies the whole perfectly; temperament pulls us one way or another, so few deeds unite both virtues at once.

sentences.zh: 如必對其優劣，則仁以枝葉扶疏為大，孝以心體本根為先，可無訟也。

translations.literal: If you must contrast their superiority and inferiority, then benevolence takes luxuriant branches and leaves as greater, filial piety takes heart and trunk as prior—there need be no lawsuit over it.
translations.idiomatic: If we must rank them, benevolence is the spreading canopy and filial piety the deep root—yet there is no real quarrel between them.

sentences.zh: 或謂先孝後仁，非仲尼序回、參之意。

translations.literal: Some say putting filial piety before benevolence is not the meaning of Confucius’ ordering of Hui and Shen.
translations.idiomatic: Critics say “filial first” cannot be what Confucius meant when he paired Yan Hui with Zeng Shen.

sentences.zh: [一０]蓋以為仁孝同質而生，純體之者，則互以為稱，虞舜、顏回是也。

translations.literal: Note [10] I take it that benevolence and filial piety are of one substance by birth; those who embody the pure whole call them interchangeably—Shun and Yan Hui are such.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: In a fully rounded character the two merge—think of Shun or Yan Hui.

sentences.zh: [一一]若偏而體之，則各有其目，公劉、曾參是也。

translations.literal: Note [11] If one embodies them partially, each has its label—Gong Liu and Zeng Shen are such.
translations.idiomatic: Where the nature is partial, one name stands out—Gong Liu for benevolence, Zeng Shen for filial piety.

sentences.zh: [一二]夫曾、閔以孝悌為至德，[一三]管仲以九合為仁功，[一四] 未有論德不先回、參，考功不大夷吾。

translations.literal: Note [12] Zeng and Min took filial fraternal duty as supreme virtue; Note [13] Guan Zhong took the nine assemblies as the achievement of benevolence; Note [14] yet never in discussing virtue is Hui not put before Shen, nor in weighing merit is Yi not ranked below Guan.
translations.idiomatic: Zeng Shen and Min Sun were paragons of family duty; Guan Zhong’s nine leagues were hailed as benevolence—yet whenever scholars weigh moral character they still pair Yan Hui with Zeng Shen at the top, and whenever they reckon public service they still set no one above Guan Zhong of Qi.

sentences.zh: 以此而言，各從其稱者也。」

translations.literal: Speaking from this, each follows its proper title.”
translations.idiomatic: Each virtue keeps its own title, and that is enough.”

sentences.zh: 注[一]辯，爭也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Bian means “dispute.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Here bian means “debate” or “contention.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]代，更也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Dai means “alternation.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Dai means “to take turns” or “replace.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]篤，厚也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Du (here) means “thick” or “solid.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The character for his name also means “solid” or “weighty.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]二致，仁、孝也。

translations.literal: Note [4] The two goals are benevolence and filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “The two goals” are benevolence and filial piety.

sentences.zh: 易系詞曰「殊塗而同歸，百慮而一致」也。

translations.literal: The Changes, Xici, says “different roads with one return, hundred cares with one outcome.”
translations.idiomatic: The Appended Remarks speak of many paths leading to one end.

sentences.zh: 注[五]較猶略也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Jiao means roughly “outline” or “compare.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Jiao here means “to sketch” or “compare in outline.”

sentences.zh: 注[六]說文曰：「縟，繁彩飾也。」

translations.literal: Note [6] The Shuowen says: “Ru means elaborate colored ornament.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Shuowen defines ru as rich, many-colored decoration.

sentences.zh: 注[七]四體謂手足也。

translations.literal: Note [7] The four limbs mean hands and feet.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Four limbs” means arms and legs.

sentences.zh: 注[八]左氏傳趙簡子問子太叔：「何謂禮？

translations.literal: Note [8] The Zuo Tradition: Viscount Jian of Zhao asked Grand Shu: “What is called ritual?”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The line echoes Grand Shu’s answer to Zhao Jianzi in the Zuo Tradition.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「聞諸先大夫子產曰：『夫禮，天之經也，地之義也，人之行也。

translations.literal: ” He answered: “I have heard from my late father, the Grand Officer Zichan, who said: ‘Ritual is the warp of Heaven, the rightness of Earth, and the conduct of man.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Shu quoted Zichan: ritual is the cosmic pattern for human conduct.

sentences.zh: 天地之經，人實則之，則天之明，因地之性。

translations.literal: The warp of Heaven and Earth—man in truth models them, then clarifies Heaven’s brightness and follows Earth’s nature.’”
translations.idiomatic: Men should mirror heaven and earth and so clarify what is bright in nature.

sentences.zh: 』」孔子取為孝經之詞也。

translations.literal: ”” Confucius took these words for the Classic of Filial Piety.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius borrowed this wording for the opening of the Classic of Filial Piety.

sentences.zh: 注[九]論語載有若之詞也。

translations.literal: Note [9] The Analects records the words of You Ruo.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The remark on the root of benevolence comes from You Ruo in the Analects.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]論語孔子曰：「參也魯，回也其庶乎？

translations.literal: Note [10] The Analects: Confucius said: “Shen is dull; Hui—he is almost there?”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Confucius called Zeng Shen slow and praised Yan Hui’s nearness to the Way.

sentences.zh: 」言庶幾於善道也。

translations.literal: ” It means he was almost on the good path.
translations.idiomatic: “Almost there” means close to moral perfection.

sentences.zh: 魯，鈍也。

translations.literal: Lu means “dull.”
translations.idiomatic: “Dull” (lu) means slow of wit.

sentences.zh: 言若先孝後仁，則曾參不得不賢於顏子。

translations.literal: To say that if filial piety comes first then benevolence comes after would mean Zeng Shen could not but be worthier than Yanzi.
translations.idiomatic: If filial piety always ranked above benevolence, Zeng Shen would outshine Yan Hui—yet Confucius ranked them the other way.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]虞舜、顏回純德既備，或仁或孝，但隨其所稱爾。

translations.literal: Note [11] Shun and Yan Hui, their pure virtue being complete, were called benevolent or filial according to whichever title fit.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Shun and Yan Hui earned either name because their virtue was entire.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]史記，公劉，後稷曾孫也。

translations.literal: Note [12] The Records: Gong Liu was great-grandson of Hou Ji.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Shiji makes Gong Liu a great-grandson of the grain god Hou Ji.

sentences.zh: 能修復後稷之業，務耕種，行地宜，百姓懷之，多從而保歸焉。

translations.literal: He was able to restore Hou Ji’s work, devoted himself to plowing and sowing, followed the land’s fitness, the hundred surnames cherished him, and many followed him to shelter and return.
translations.idiomatic: He revived agriculture, suited crops to soil, and drew the people to follow him into exile.

sentences.zh: 故公劉以仁紀德，曾參以至孝稱賢，此則各自為目，不能總兼其美也。

translations.literal: Therefore Gong Liu is recorded for benevolence, Zeng Shen for supreme filial piety as worthiness—thus each has its separate heading and cannot sum up the other’s excellence.
translations.idiomatic: So Gong Liu stands for public benevolence and Zeng Shen for private devotion—each label fits only one side.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]曾參、閔損也。

translations.literal: Note [13] Zeng Shen and Min Sun.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Zeng Shen and Min Sun (Ziqian).

sentences.zh: 注[一四]論語孔子曰：「桓公九合諸侯，不以兵車，管仲之力，如其仁，如其仁。

translations.literal: Note [14] The Analects: Confucius said: “Duke Huan assembled the lords nine times without using chariots of war—it was Guan Zhong’s power; as his benevolence, as his benevolence.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Confucius credited Guan Zhong’s “benevolence” with Duke Huan’s nine leagues without war.

sentences.zh: 」九合者，謂再會於鄄，兩會於幽，又會檉、首止、戴寧、母洮、葵丘也。

translations.literal: ” The nine assemblies mean assembling twice at Juan, twice at You, and again at Zhi, Shouzhi, Dai, Ning, Mutao, and Kuiqiu.
translations.idiomatic: The “nine” leagues are the gatherings at Juan (twice), You (twice), Zhi, Shouzhi, Dai, Ning, Mutao, and Kuiqiu.

sentences.zh: 前越巂太守李文德素善於篤，時在京師，謂公卿曰：「延叔堅有王佐之才，奈何屈千里之足乎？

translations.literal: The former Administrator of Yuexi, Li Wende, had always been on good terms with Du; at that time he was in the capital and said to the high ministers: “Yan Shujian has talent fit for a king’s assistant—why hobble a steed meant to run a thousand li?”
translations.idiomatic: Li Wende, once governor of Yuexi and an old friend, told the high ministers in Luoyang that Yan Du had the makings of a chief minister and should not be left in the provinces.

sentences.zh: 」欲令引進之。

translations.literal: ” He wished to have him recommended and brought forward.
translations.idiomatic: He wanted them to pull strings and bring Yan Du back to court.

sentences.zh: 篤聞，乃為書止文德曰：「夫道之將廢，所謂命也。

translations.literal: Du heard of it and wrote a letter to stop Wende, saying: “When the Way is about to fall into ruin, that is what is called fate.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du answered with a letter: “When the Way is failing, men call it fate.

sentences.zh: [一]流聞乃欲相為求還東觀，來命雖篤，所未敢當。

translations.literal: Note [1] Hearing the rumor, you wished to seek my return to the Eastern Pavilion; your charge, though earnest, is what I dare not accept.
translations.idiomatic: I hear you would lobby to send me back to the Eastern Library; I am grateful but cannot accept.

sentences.zh: 吾嘗昧爽櫛梳，坐於客堂。

translations.literal: I have sat at dawn combing my hair in the guest hall.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn I comb my hair and sit in the guest hall.

sentences.zh: [二]朝則誦羲、文之易，虞、夏之書，歷公旦之典禮，覽仲尼之春秋。

translations.literal: Note [2] In the morning I chant the Changes of Xi and Wen, the Documents of Yu and Xia, the rituals of the Duke of Zhou, and peruse the Spring and Autumn of Zhongni.
translations.idiomatic: Mornings I read the Changes, the earliest Documents, the Zhou rituals, and Confucius’ Spring and Autumn.

sentences.zh: [三] 夕則消搖內階，詠詩南軒。

translations.literal: Note [3] In the evening I wander at ease on the inner steps and chant poems by the south window.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Evenings I stroll the inner court and hum poems by the south casement.

sentences.zh: [四]百家觿氏，投閒而作。

translations.literal: Note [4] The hundred schools and the Di clan—I seize leisure and compose.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: In spare moments I turn to the masters and the philosophers.

sentences.zh: [五]洋洋乎其盈耳也，[六] 渙爛兮其溢目也，[七]紛紛欣欣兮其獨樂也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Vast and full it fills my ears; Note [6] dazzling it floods my eyes; Note [7] riotous and glad is the joy I find alone.
translations.idiomatic: The sound fills my ears, the color floods my eyes, and a private joy runs riot in me.

sentences.zh: 當此之時，不知天之為蓋，地之為輿；

translations.literal: At such a time I do not know that Heaven is a cover and Earth a carriage;
translations.idiomatic: In those hours I forget whether heaven is a dome or earth a cart;

sentences.zh: [八]不知世之有人，己之有軀也。

translations.literal: Note [8] I do not know that the world has other men or that I have a body.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: I forget there is a world beyond me or a body that belongs to me.

sentences.zh: 雖漸離擊築，傍若無人，[九]高鳳讀書，不知暴雨，[一０]方之於吾，未足況也。

translations.literal: Though Jianli strikes the zhu as if no one else were there, Note [9] and Gao Feng reads his books unaware of the cloudburst, Note [10] set beside me they are not enough for a comparison.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Jianli could thrum his zhu as if the market were empty, and Gao Feng could read through a thunderstorm—yet neither example comes close to the absorption I describe.

sentences.zh: 且吾自束修已來，[一一]為人臣不陷於不忠，為人子不陷於不孝，上交不諂，下交不黷，[一二]從此而歿，下見先君遠祖，可不籩赧。

translations.literal: Moreover, since I bound my hair for study, Note [11] as a subject I have not fallen into disloyalty, as a son I have not fallen into unfilial conduct; in dealing with those above I do not flatter, with those below I do not profane, Note [12]—if I die from this point and go down to meet my late lords and remote ancestors, need I blush?
translations.idiomatic: Since I came of age I have kept faith as a minister and piety as a son; I have neither truckled to the great nor trampled the humble. When I meet my forebears below, I shall not hang my head for shame.

sentences.zh: [一三]如此而不以善止者，恐如教羿射者也。

translations.literal: Note [13] If having reached this I still do not stop at what is good, I fear it would be like teaching Yi archery.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: To push further would be like the fable of teaching the master archer Yi to shoot.

sentences.zh: [一四]慎勿迷其本，□其生也。

translations.literal: Note [14] Take care not to lose the root and □ your life.”
translations.idiomatic: Guard your true vocation and do not throw your life away on ambition.”

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]論語孔子曰：「道之將行也與？

translations.literal: ” Note [1] The Analects: Confucius said: “When the Way is about to prevail—is it not
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Confucius said in the Analects, “If the Way is going to triumph—is it not—

sentences.zh: 命也。

translations.literal: fate?
translations.idiomatic: —a matter of fate?”

sentences.zh: 道之將廢也與？

translations.literal: When the Way is about to fall—is it not
translations.idiomatic: “If the Way is going to fail—is it not—

sentences.zh: 命也。」

translations.literal: fate?”
translations.idiomatic: —a matter of fate?”

sentences.zh: 注[二]孔安國注尚書曰：「昧，暝也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Kong Anguo’s commentary on the Documents says: “Mei means twilight.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Kong Anguo glosses mei as dusk.

sentences.zh: 爽，明也。」

translations.literal: Shuang means dawn.”
translations.idiomatic: Shuang means first light.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]周公攝政七年，制禮作樂。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Duke of Zhou held the regency seven years, then established ritual and composed music.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Duke of Zhou ruled as regent for seven years and then fixed the rites and music.

sentences.zh: 班固東都賦曰「今論者但知誦虞、夏之書，詠殷、周之詩，講羲、文之易，論孔氏之春秋」也。

translations.literal: Ban Gu’s Eastern Capital rhapsody says “today’s debaters only know to chant the books of Yu and Xia,” and so on.
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu’s rhapsody on the eastern capital lists the same canon Yan Du claims to read each morning.

sentences.zh: 注[四]楚詞：「高堂邃宇，鏤檻層軒。

translations.literal: Note [4] Songs of Chu: “Lofty halls and deep chambers, carved balustrades and storied windows.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: A Chu ci couplet describes a lofty hall with latticed galleries.

sentences.zh: 」王逸注云：「軒，樓板也。」

translations.literal: ” Wang Yi’s commentary says: “Xuan means the plank flooring of a tower.”
translations.idiomatic: Wang Yi glosses xuan as the boarded gallery of an upper story.

sentences.zh: 注[五]言誦經典之餘，投射閒隙而翫百氏也。

translations.literal: Note [5] It means that besides chanting the classics he seizes leisure moments to peruse the hundred schools.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Between sessions with the canon he fills the gaps by browsing miscellaneous writers.

sentences.zh: 注[六]洋洋，美也。

translations.literal: Note [6] Yangyang means beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Yangyang here means rich and full.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「洋洋乎盈耳哉。」

translations.literal: The Analects says: “How rich and full—it fills the ear!”
translations.idiomatic: The Analects praises music that floods the listener’s ear.

sentences.zh: 注[七]渙爛，文章貌也。

translations.literal: Note [7] Huanlan describes the appearance of patterned writing.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Huanlan describes brilliant, well-wrought prose.

sentences.zh: 注[八]宋玉大言賦曰「方地為輿，員天為蓋」也。

translations.literal: Note [8] Song Yu’s Great Words rhapsody says “we square the earth to make a carriage and round the sky to make a canopy.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Song Yu’s rhapsody imagines the earth as a chariot floor and heaven as a domed cover.

sentences.zh: 注[九]說文曰：「築，五弦之樂也。

translations.literal: Note [9] The Shuowen says: “Zhu is a five-stringed instrument.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Shuowen defines the zhu as a five-stringed lute.

sentences.zh: 」沈約宋書曰：「築不知誰所造也。

translations.literal: ” Shen Yue’s Song shu says: “No one knows who first made the zhu.
translations.idiomatic: The Song shu admits that the zhu’s inventor is unknown.

sentences.zh: 史記唯雲高漸離擊築。

translations.literal: The Records only says Gao Jianli struck the zhu.
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji mentions only Gao Jianli playing it.

sentences.zh: 」案：今築形似箏，有項有柱。

translations.literal: ” Investigation: today’s zhu resembles the zheng, with a neck and bridges.
translations.idiomatic: Modern instruments shaped like the zheng retain neck and bridges.

sentences.zh: 史記，荊軻至燕，日與屠狗及高漸離擊築，荊軻和而歌於巿中，相樂，已而相泣，傍若無人。

translations.literal: The Records: when Jing Ke reached Yan he daily drank with dog-butcher friends and Gao Jianli striking the zhu; Jing Ke harmonized and sang in the market, first merry then weeping, as if no one else were there.
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji tells how Jing Ke, the dog-butcher, and Gao Jianli sang in the Yan capital—first laughing, then weeping—as if the crowd did not exist.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]事具逸人傳也。

translations.literal: Note [10] The affair is fully set out in the Traditions of Recluses.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gao Feng’s storm story appears in the recluse biographies.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]束修謂束帶修飾。

translations.literal: Note [11] “Binding the hair for study” means girding the sash and grooming oneself for learning.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Hair bound for study” means the age when a youth girds himself for formal education.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注論語曰「謂年十五已上」也。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan’s commentary on the Analects says “it means fifteen sui and above.”
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan takes it to mean fifteen years and older.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]易系詞之文也。

translations.literal: Note [12] It is text from the Changes, Xici.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The line about not flattering above or slighting below comes from the Appended Remarks.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]色媿曰赧，音女板反。

translations.literal: Note [13] Color-shame is called nan, read nu ban fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Nan means flushing with shame; the fanqie is 女板反.

sentences.zh: 注[一四]史記，有養由基者，善射者也，去柳葉百步而射之，百發而百中之。

translations.literal: Note [14] The Records: there was a Yang Youji who excelled at archery; he shot at willow leaves a hundred paces away, a hundred shots and a hundred hits.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Shiji tells of Yang Youji, who could hit a willow leaf a hundred paces off, a hundred times running.

sentences.zh: 左右觀者數千人，皆曰「善射」。

translations.literal: Several thousand onlookers to left and right all said “fine shooting.”
translations.idiomatic: Thousands of spectators cried, “A master bowman!”

sentences.zh: 有一人立其旁，曰：「善，可教射矣。

translations.literal: One man stood beside him and said: “Good—he can be taught to shoot.”
translations.idiomatic: A stranger beside him said, “Skillful—but still teachable.”

sentences.zh: 」養由基怒，釋弓搤□曰：「客安能教我射乎？

translations.literal: ” Yang Youji grew angry, laid down his bow and gripped his □ saying: “How can a traveler teach me to shoot?”
translations.idiomatic: Yang Youji dropped his bow, seized the bridge of his nose, and snapped, “What right has a passer-by to instruct me?”

sentences.zh: 」客曰：「非吾能教枝左詘右也。

translations.literal: ” The traveler said: “It is not that I can teach ‘branch left and bend right.’
translations.idiomatic: The man answered, “I am not teaching you fancy footwork.

sentences.zh: 夫去柳葉百步而射之，百發百中之，不以善息，少焉氣衰力倦，弓撥矢鉤，一發不中者百發盡息。

translations.literal: To shoot willow leaves a hundred paces off with a hundred hits and not rest on that excellence—presently breath fails and strength tires, the bow warps and arrows hook, and one miss undoes the credit of a hundred hits.”
translations.idiomatic: If you never pause after a perfect hundred, your wind soon fails, your bow twists, and one miss wipes out the whole string of bulls-eyes.”

sentences.zh: 」此言羿者，蓋以俱善射而稱之焉。

translations.literal: ” This mention of Yi is probably because both were famed for archery.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The parable is tagged with Yi’s name because both men were legendary archers.

sentences.zh: 後遭黨事禁錮。

translations.literal: Later he encountered the partisan proscription and was immured.
translations.idiomatic: Later the partisan persecutions caught him and he was barred from office.

sentences.zh: [一]永康元年，卒於家。

translations.literal: Note [1] In the first year of Yongkang he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home in the first year of Yongkang (167).

sentences.zh: 鄉里圖其形於屈原之廟。

translations.literal: His home district painted his likeness in Qu Yuan’s temple.
translations.idiomatic: His neighbors hung his portrait in the temple of Qu Yuan.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]錮謂閉塞。

translations.literal: Note [1] Gu means to block and shut.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gu here means “locked out” or “proscribed.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]屈原，楚大夫，抱忠貞而死。

translations.literal: Note [2] Qu Yuan was a great officer of Chu who died holding loyalty and purity.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Qu Yuan, loyal minister of Chu, drowned himself for principle.

sentences.zh: 篤有志行文彩，故圖其像而偶之焉。

translations.literal: Du had determination, conduct, and literary polish, so they paired his image with Qu’s.
translations.idiomatic: They paired Yan Du with Qu Yuan because he matched that blend of integrity and letters.

sentences.zh: 篤論解經傳，多所駁正，後儒服虔等以為折中。

translations.literal: Du’s treatises explaining the classics often refuted and corrected received readings; later scholars such as Fu Qian took them as the balanced view.
translations.idiomatic: His essays on the canon overturned many sloppy glosses, and later exegetes such as Fu Qian treated them as authoritative.

sentences.zh: 所著詩、論、銘、書、應訊、表、教令，[一]凡二十篇雲。

translations.literal: What he wrote—poems, treatises, inscriptions, letters, replies to questions, memorials, and edicts, Note [1]—comes to twenty pieces in all.
translations.idiomatic: His surviving works—verse, essays, inscriptions, letters, dialogues, memorials, and commands—fill twenty titles.

sentences.zh: 注[一]訊，問也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Xun means “question.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Xun means a formal question or interrogation.

sentences.zh: 蓋荅客難之類。

translations.literal: In general they belong to the class of “Answering the Guest’s Difficulties.”
translations.idiomatic: They belong to the same genre as “Answering a Guest’s Cavils.”

sentences.zh: 史弼字公謙，陳留考城人也。

translations.literal: Shi Bi, courtesy name Gongqian, was a man of Kaocheng in Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi, whose courtesy name was Gongqian, came from Kaocheng in Chenliu.

sentences.zh: 父敞，順帝時以佞辯至尚書、郡守。

translations.literal: His father Chang, in Shundi’s time by glib persuasion rose to Minister and commandery governor.
translations.idiomatic: His father Shi Chang rose under Emperor Shun to Minister in the capital and governor of a commandery through clever tongue.

sentences.zh: [一]弼少篤學，聚徒數百。

translations.literal: Note [1] Bi in youth studied intently and gathered several hundred disciples.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Shi Bi studied to the point of austerity and drew hundreds of students.

sentences.zh: 仕州郡，[二]辟公府，遷北軍中候。

translations.literal: He served commandery and province, Note [2] was summoned to the high ministries, and was promoted to colonel of the Northern Army.
translations.idiomatic: He held provincial posts, entered the ministries, and became colonel of the Northern Army.

sentences.zh: 注[一]續漢書曰「敞為京兆尹，化有能名，尤善條教，見稱於三輔」也。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Continued Han Documents says “Chang was Metropolitan Governor of the capital region, famed for ability, especially for clear regulations, praised in the three adjuncts.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Xù Hànshū praises Shi Chang as a capable Metropolitan Governor with a gift for lucid directives.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰：「弼年二十為郡功曹，承前太守宋欣穢濁之後，悉條諸生聚斂奸吏百餘人，皆白太守，埽多還縣，高名由此而興。」

translations.literal: Note [2] Xie Cheng’s book says: “At twenty Bi was merit clerk of the commandery; following a former Administrator Song Xin who had left the office corrupt, he drew up a full list of more than a hundred licentious students and venal clerks, reported all to the Administrator, swept many back to their counties, and his high reputation rose from this.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Xie Cheng records that at twenty he cleaned house after a corrupt governor, cashiering over a hundred crooked scholars and clerks and winning instant fame.

sentences.zh: 注[二]梁孝王，景帝弟，竇太后少子，愛之，賜天子旌旗，出警入蹕。

translations.literal: Note [2] Prince Xiao of Liang was Emperor Jing’s younger brother, the youngest son of Empress Dowager Dou, whom she loved; he was given the imperial pennons and escort with cleared roads.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Emperor Jing’s brother Liu Wu received princely regalia almost equal to the throne.

sentences.zh: 景帝嘗與王宴太后前，曰：「千秋萬歲後傳王。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing once feasted with the prince before the empress dowager and said: “After a thousand autumns and myriad years I will pass the throne to you.”
translations.idiomatic: At a banquet before their mother Emperor Jing promised Liu Wu the succession.

sentences.zh: 」爰盎諫不許，遂令人刺殺盎也。

translations.literal: ” Yuan Ang remonstrated and it was not allowed, so he had someone stab Yuan Ang to death.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Ang’s protest blocked the plan, so the prince had Yuan murdered.

sentences.zh: 注[三]剽，悍也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Piao means fierce.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Piao means fierce or predatory.

sentences.zh: 逞，快也。

translations.literal: Cheng means gratified.
translations.idiomatic: Cheng means gratified or vented.

sentences.zh: 謂被侵枉不快之人也。

translations.literal: It refers to people wronged and unable to feel satisfaction.
translations.idiomatic: The phrase describes men who feel wronged and cannot rest.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「率腢不逞之人。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: “Lead discontented men who will not be appeased.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Zuo Tradition warns against gathering malcontents who nurse unresolved grievances.

sentences.zh: 」剽音疋妙反。

translations.literal: ” Piao is read piao miao fan.
translations.idiomatic: The graph glossed for “fierce” is read in the entering tone with the fanqie spelling given in the commentary.

sentences.zh: 注[四]有虛言無實行也。

translations.literal: Note [4] It means boasting speech without solid conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: That is, big talk without deeds to match.

sentences.zh: 注[五]前書羊勝勸梁王求漢嗣，伍被勸准南*

translations.literal: Note [5] In the Former Documents Yang Sheng urged the Prince of Liang to seek the Han heir; Wu Bei urged the Prince of Huainan*
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The gloss cites Yang Sheng’s flattery of Liu Wu and Wu Bei’s incitement of the Huainan heir—

translations.literal: (zi)
translations.idiomatic: The lacuna in the manuscript is filled with “heir,” i.e., the crown prince of Huainan.

sentences.zh: **[王]*謀反誅也。

translations.literal: **[wang]* to rebel and be executed.
translations.idiomatic: —both princes who listened to sycophants and came to ruin.

sentences.zh: 注[六]友，親也。

translations.literal: Note [6] You means intimate.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: You here means kin or intimate ally.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰：「惟孝友于兄弟。」

translations.literal: The Documents says: “Be filially pious and friendly toward brothers.”
translations.idiomatic: The Documents enjoin brotherly harmony as part of filial duty.

sentences.zh: 注[七]滋，長；

translations.literal: Note [7] Zi means to grow;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Zi means to sprout or grow.

sentences.zh: 蔓，延也。

translations.literal: man means to spread.
translations.idiomatic: Man means to spread unchecked.

sentences.zh: 左氏傳：「無使滋蔓，蔓難圖也。」

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition: “Do not let it grow and spread; once spread it is hard to deal with.”
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition warns, “Do not let evil sprawl—sprawl is hard to uproot.”

sentences.zh: 弼遷尚書，出為平原相。

translations.literal: Bi was promoted to Minister, then went out as Administrator of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi became a Minister, then left the capital as governor of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 時詔書下舉鉤黨，[一]郡國所奏相連及者多至數百，唯弼獨無所上。

translations.literal: At that time an edict went down ordering the arrest of partisan connections; Note [1] what commanderies and kingdoms reported, linking people in chains, often ran to several hundreds—only Bi alone sent up nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Edicts ordered round-ups of the “partisan” networks; most commanderies denounced hundreds of names, but Pingyuan filed none.

sentences.zh: 詔書前後切□州郡，[二]髡笞掾史。

translations.literal: Edicts repeatedly pressed the provinces harshly, Note [2] shaving and flogging clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial messengers scourged provincial staff to force names.

sentences.zh: 從事坐傳責曰：[三]「詔書疾惡黨人，旨意懇惻。

translations.literal: The attendant clerk, sitting in the post-house, rebuked him: Note [3] “The edict hates partisans; the intent is earnest.
translations.idiomatic: An inspector met him at the relay inn and demanded, “The court despises factions; the order is urgent.

sentences.zh: 青州六郡，其五有黨，[四]近國甘陵，亦考南北部，[五] 平原何理而得獨無？

translations.literal: Of the six commanderies of Qingzhou, five have partisans; Note [4] near the capital Ganling too was examined for north and south factions; Note [5] by what principle does Pingyuan alone have none?”
translations.idiomatic: Five of Qingzhou’s six circuits show lists; even Ganling by the capital split into northern and southern cliques—how can Pingyuan claim innocence?”

sentences.zh: 」弼曰：「先王疆理天下，畫界分境，[六]水土異齊，風俗不同，[七]它郡自有，平原自無，胡可相比？

translations.literal: ” Bi said: “The former kings mapped and ordered the realm, drawing boundaries and dividing territories; Note [6] water and soil differ in fitness, customs differ, Note [7] other commanderies may have them—Pingyuan by nature has none—how can they be compared?
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi answered, “The sages mapped soil and custom: what thrives in Qi need not exist in Pingyuan. Other regions may breed factions; ours does not. You cannot equate them.

sentences.zh: 若承望上司，誣陷良善，淫刑濫罰，以逞非理，則平原之人，戶可為黨。

translations.literal: If I curry favor with superiors, slander the good, apply wanton punishments and excessive penalties to vent what is unjust, then every household in Pingyuan could be called partisan.
translations.idiomatic: If I fawned on you, framed the innocent, and tortured people to please you, I could mark every door in the county a “faction.”

sentences.zh: 相有死而已，所不能也。

translations.literal: As administrator I have only death—I cannot do that.”
translations.idiomatic: I would sooner die than sign such a list.”

sentences.zh: 」從事大怒，即收郡僚職送獄，道舉奏弼。

translations.literal: The attendant clerk was greatly angered, at once seized the commandery staff and sent them to prison, and on the road memorialized against Bi.
translations.idiomatic: The inspector jailed his aides and sent a denunciation of Shi Bi to Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 會黨禁中解，弼以俸贖罪得免，[八]濟活者千餘人。

translations.literal: When the partisan ban was partly lifted, Bi redeemed his offense with salary, Note [8] and more than a thousand people he had saved lived.
translations.idiomatic: When the proscription eased, he bought off the charge with his pay, and over a thousand people he had shielded survived.

sentences.zh: 注[一]鉤謂相連也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Gou means to link in chains.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gou means “to hook” or link names together.

sentences.zh: 注[二]切，急也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Qie means urgent.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Qie means pressing or severe.

sentences.zh: □，退也。

translations.literal: □ means to yield or draw back.
translations.idiomatic: The glossed character means to give way or retreat.

sentences.zh: 注[三]續漢志每州皆有從事史及諸曹掾史。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Continued Han Treatise says each province has attendant clerks and the various bureau clerks.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Xù Hàn zhi lists the provincial inspector’s staff.

sentences.zh: 傳，客舍也，音知戀反。

translations.literal: Zhuan means a traveler’s inn, read zhi luan fan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan is a post-station; the fanqie is 知戀反.

sentences.zh: 坐傳捨召弼而責。

translations.literal: He sat in the post-house inn and summoned Bi to rebuke him.
translations.idiomatic: The man called Shi Bi to the relay station for a dressing-down.

sentences.zh: 注[四]濟南、樂安、齊國、東萊、平原、北海六郡，青州所管也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Jinan, Le’an, Qi, Donglai, Pingyuan, and Beihai—the six commanderies governed by Qingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Qingzhou supervised those six commanderies.

sentences.zh: 青州在齊國臨淄，見漢官儀。

translations.literal: Qingzhou’s seat was at Linzi in Qi; see the Han guan yi.
translations.idiomatic: Its capital was Linzi in Qi, per the Han guan yi.

sentences.zh: 注[五]桓帝為蠡吾侯，受學於甘陵周福，及帝即位，擢福為尚書。

translations.literal: Note [5] Emperor Huan, as marquis of Liwu, studied under Zhou Fu of Ganling; when he ascended the throne he promoted Fu to Minister.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Emperor Huan had studied under Zhou Fu of Ganling and raised him to high office.

sentences.zh: 時同郡河南尹房植有名當朝，二家賓客互相譏揣，遂各樹朋徒，漸成尤隙，由是甘陵有南北部。

translations.literal: At that time Fang Zhi of the same commandery, Metropolitan Governor of Henan, had fame at court; the guests of the two houses mocked one another, each raised its own faction, gradually forming deep rancor, so Ganling had north and south factions.
translations.idiomatic: Fang Zhi of Henan rivaled Zhou Fu; their clients traded insults until Ganling split into northern and southern camps.

sentences.zh: 見黨人篇序也。

translations.literal: See the preface to the partisan chapter.
translations.idiomatic: The full story opens the treatise on the partisans.

sentences.zh: 注[六]疆，界也。

translations.literal: Note [6] Jiang means boundary.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Jiang means a territorial limit.

sentences.zh: 理，正也。

translations.literal: Li means to set in order.
translations.idiomatic: Li means to arrange or regulate.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「先王疆理天下，物土之宜而布其利」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says “the former kings mapped and ordered the realm, assessed the fitness of soil, and spread its benefits.”
