translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition praises early kings who “mapped the realm and assigned its gains.”

sentences.zh: 注[七]前書曰「凡人函五常之性，而其剛柔緩急，音聲不同。

translations.literal: Note [7] The Former Documents says “all men embody the five constants, yet their hardness and softness, slowness and haste, and voices differ.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Hanshu notes that all men share the five virtues yet differ in temperament and speech.

sentences.zh: 系水土之風氣，故謂之風。

translations.literal: They are tied to the wind and qi of water and soil—therefore it is called feng.
translations.idiomatic: What comes from terrain and climate is called feng (“wind”).

sentences.zh: 好惡取捨，動靜無常，隨君上之情慾，故謂之俗」也。

translations.literal: Likes and dislikes, taking and leaving, movement and rest without constancy, following the ruler’s passions—therefore it is called su.”
translations.idiomatic: Habits that follow a lord’s whims are called su (“custom”).

sentences.zh: 注[八]*

translations.literal: Note [8]*
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Note eight (the gloss continues on the following fragment).

translations.literal: (feng)
translations.idiomatic: The variant character in the margin is the one normally read “to receive” or “tribute.”

sentences.zh: **[俸]*音扶用反。

translations.literal: **[feng]* is read feng yong fan.
translations.idiomatic: The word for “salary” in the text is read with the fanqie spelling 扶用反.

sentences.zh: 弼為政特挫抑強豪，其小民有罪，多所容貸。

translations.literal: In governing, Bi especially curbed the powerful magnates; when commoners committed offenses he often indulged and excused them.
translations.idiomatic: As governor he humbled the great families while forgiving petty folk who broke the law.

sentences.zh: 遷河東太守，被一切詔書當舉孝廉。

translations.literal: He was transferred to Administrator of Hedong and received a blanket edict ordering him to recommend Filial and Incorrupt candidates.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted to governor of Hedong, he was hit with a general edict demanding fresh Filial-and-Incorrupt nominations.

sentences.zh: 弼知多權貴請托，乃豫□斷絕書屬。

translations.literal: Bi knew there would be many requests from the powerful, so in advance he □ cut off letters of connection.
translations.idiomatic: Expecting a flood of patronage letters from the great families, he shut his door to all written solicitations beforehand.

sentences.zh: [一]中常侍侯覽果遣諸生繼書請之，並求假鹽稅，積日不得通。

translations.literal: Note [1] The palace attendant Hou Lan indeed sent students with letters in succession asking for favors, and also sought a lease on the salt tax; days piled up without getting through.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuch Hou Lan sent scholars with back-to-back letters—and a demand for the salt monopoly—yet could not get past the yamen gate.

sentences.zh: 生乃說以它事謁弼，而因達覽書。

translations.literal: The student then pleaded another matter to visit Bi, and thereby delivered Lan’s letter.
translations.idiomatic: One student invented a pretext for an audience and slipped Hou Lan’s note onto the desk.

sentences.zh: 弼大怒曰：「太守忝荷重任，當選士報國，爾何人而偽詐無狀！

translations.literal: Bi grew greatly angry and said: “The Administrator disgracefully bears a heavy charge—he must select gentlemen to repay the state; what manner of man are you, false and shameless!
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi thundered, “I wear the seal to serve the throne, not to traffic in names—who are you to peddle fraud in my hall?”

sentences.zh: 」命左右引出，楚捶數百，府丞、掾史十餘人皆諫於廷，弼不對。

translations.literal: ” He ordered those at his left and right to lead him out and Chu-style flog him several hundred times; more than ten assistant magistrates and clerks all remonstrated in the court, but Bi did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: He had the man dragged out and flogged hundreds of times while a dozen aides pleaded in the courtyard; he gave no reply.

sentences.zh: 遂付安邑獄，即日考殺之。

translations.literal: He then handed him over to Anyi prison and the same day examined and killed him.
translations.idiomatic: He sent the wretch to Anyi jail, tried him the same day, and had him executed.

sentences.zh: 侯覽大怨，遂詐作飛章下司隸，誣弼誹謗，檻車征。

translations.literal: Hou Lan nursed a great grudge, then forged a flying memorial down to the Director of Retainers, slandering Bi with defamation, and summoned him in a caged cart.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Lan forged an urgent denunciation to the capital police, accusing Shi Bi of slander, and had him hauled north in a prison wagon.

sentences.zh: 吏人莫敢近者，唯前孝廉裴瑜送到崤澠之閒，大言於道傍曰：「明府摧折虐臣，選德報國，如其獲罪，足以垂名竹帛，願不憂不懼。

translations.literal: No clerks or people dared draw near; only the former Filial and Incorrupt Pei Yu escorted him to the region between Xiao and Mian, and spoke loudly beside the road, saying: “My lord has broken a cruel minister and chosen virtue to repay the state; if you suffer punishment for it, it is enough to inscribe your name on bamboo and silk—please do not worry or fear.
translations.idiomatic: Only Pei Yu, a former nominee, walked with him to the Xiao–Mian pass and shouted for all to hear, “You crushed a tyrant for the Han—if you pay with your life, history will remember you; walk on without fear.”

sentences.zh: 」弼曰：「『誰謂荼苦，其甘如薺。

translations.literal: ” Bi said: “‘Who says the sowthistle is bitter—its taste is sweet as shepherd’s purse.’
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi answered, “They say the sowthistle is bitter, yet it can taste sweet as shepherd’s purse.”

sentences.zh: 』[二]昔人刎頸，九死不恨。

translations.literal: ” Note [2] Men of old cut their throats and faced nine deaths without regret.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Men of old died many deaths without regret when honor demanded it.

sentences.zh: 」[三] 及下廷尉詔獄，平原吏人奔走詣闕訟之。

translations.literal: ” Note [3] When he was sent down to the Minister of Justice’s edict prison, the clerks and people of Pingyuan ran to the palace to sue on his behalf.
translations.idiomatic: When the imperial jail took him, the whole of Pingyuan raced to Luoyang to petition for justice.

sentences.zh: 又前孝廉魏劭毀變形服，詐為家僮，瞻護於弼。

translations.literal: Also the former Filial and Incorrupt Wei Shao disfigured himself and changed his clothes, pretended to be a household slave, and watched over Bi.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Shao, another former nominee, scarred his face, dressed as a bondsman, and stayed at Shi Bi’s side.

sentences.zh: 弼遂受誣，事當□市。

translations.literal: Bi then suffered the slander; the affair called for □ execution in the market.
translations.idiomatic: The forged charge should have sent him to the public block.

sentences.zh: 劭與郡人賣郡邸，[四]行賂於侯覽，得減死罪一等，論輸左校。

translations.literal: Shao with a fellow townsman sold the commandery hostel, Note [4] bribed Hou Lan, obtained reduction of the death penalty by one degree, and was sentenced to labor in the Left Work Camp.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Shao sold a county hostel in Luoyang, bought off Hou Lan, won commutation from death, and drew a sentence to the convict workshops.

sentences.zh: 時人或譏曰：「平原行貨以免君，無乃蚩乎！

translations.literal: Some at the time mocked, saying: “Pingyuan used goods to ransom its lord—is that not shameful!
translations.idiomatic: Wits sneered that Pingyuan had “purchased” its governor’s life.

sentences.zh: 」陶丘洪曰：

translations.literal: ” Tao Qiu Hong said:
translations.idiomatic: Taoqiu Hong retorted,

sentences.zh: [五]「昔文王牖裡，閎、散懷金。

translations.literal: Note [5] “In olden days King Wen was in Youli; Hong and San carried gold.
translations.idiomatic: “When King Wen languished in Youli, Hong and San weighed out gold to free him.”

sentences.zh: [六]史弼遭患，義夫獻寶。

translations.literal: Note [6] Shi Bi met disaster; a man of duty offered treasure.
translations.idiomatic: “When Shi Bi fell into trouble, righteous men paid ransom—

sentences.zh: 亦何疑焉！

translations.literal: What doubt is there!”
translations.idiomatic: why fault Pingyuan for doing the same?”

sentences.zh: 」於是議者乃息。

translations.literal: ” Thereupon the debaters rested.
translations.idiomatic: The gossip died away.

sentences.zh: 刑竟歸田裡，稱病閉門不出。

translations.literal: When punishment ended he returned to field and village, pleaded illness, and did not go out behind closed doors.
translations.idiomatic: After his release he retired to the country, feigned illness, and never opened his gate.

sentences.zh: 數為公卿所薦，議郎何休又訟弼有干國之器，宜登台相，征拜議郎。

translations.literal: He was repeatedly recommended by dukes and ministers; Gentleman Consultant He Xiu again sued that Bi had the capacity to sustain the state and ought to mount the terrace as minister, and he was summoned and appointed Court Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers kept recommending him; He Xiu argued he had ministerial timber, and the court recalled him as a Court Gentleman.

sentences.zh: 侯覽等惡之。

translations.literal: Hou Lan and others hated him.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Lan and his clique still loathed him.

sentences.zh: 光和中，出為彭城相，會病卒。

translations.literal: In the Guanghe era he went out as chancellor of Pengcheng and died of illness in office.
translations.idiomatic: During Guanghe he was sent out as chancellor of Pengcheng and died there of illness.

sentences.zh: 裴瑜位至尚書。

translations.literal: Pei Yu rose to the rank of Minister.
translations.idiomatic: Pei Yu later reached the post of Minister.

sentences.zh: [七] 注[一]屬音之欲反。

translations.literal: Note [7] Note [1] Shu is read zhi yu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The graph for “connection” is glossed with the fanqie reading zhi-yu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩□風也。

translations.literal: Note [2] An ode from the Poetry □ Airs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The quotation comes from the Airs of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 荼，苦菜也。

translations.literal: Tu is the bitter herb.
translations.idiomatic: “Sowthistle” names a bitter edible herb.

sentences.zh: 注[三]刎，割也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Wen means to cut the throat.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Wen means to cut the throat.

sentences.zh: 楚詞曰「雖九死其猶未悔」也。

translations.literal: The Songs of Chu says “though nine deaths I still do not regret.”
translations.idiomatic: The Chuci line runs, “Nine deaths would not make me repent.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]郡邸，若今之寺邸也。

translations.literal: Note [4] A commandery hostel is like today’s temple hostels.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: A “commandery hostel” in the capital served visiting officials, like a later monastic guesthouse.

sentences.zh: 注[五]青州先賢傳曰：「洪字子林，平原人也。

translations.literal: Note [5] The Qingzhou xianxian zhuan says: “Hong’s courtesy name was Zilin; he was a man of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Qingzhou worthy biography names Taoqiu Hong, courtesy Zilin, of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 清達博辯，文冠當代。

translations.literal: Clear, penetrating, broadly argumentative, his writing crowned the age.
translations.idiomatic: He was lucid, eloquent, and the foremost writer of his day.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉，不行，辟太尉府。

translations.literal: Recommended as Filial and Incorrupt, he did not go; summoned to the Grand Commandant’s office.
translations.idiomatic: He declined nomination but accepted a summons to the Grand Commandant’s bureau.

sentences.zh: 年三十卒。」

translations.literal: He died at thirty.”
translations.idiomatic: He died at thirty.”

sentences.zh: 注[六]牖裡，殷獄名。

translations.literal: Note [6] Youli is the name of a Yin prison.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Youli was the Shang prison where King Wen was held.

sentences.zh: 或作「羑」，亦名羑城，在今相州湯陰縣北。

translations.literal: It is sometimes written “羑,” also called You city, north of Tangyin county in Xiangzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Some texts write the name as You; the site lies north of present-day Tangyin.

sentences.zh: 帝王紀：「散宜生、南宮括、閎夭學乎呂尚。

translations.literal: The Diwang ji says: “San Yisheng, Nangong Kuo, and Hong Yao studied with Lü Shang.
translations.idiomatic: The Diwang ji says San Yisheng, Nangong Kuo, and Hong Yao studied under Lü Shang.

sentences.zh: 尚知三人賢，結朋友之交。

translations.literal: Shang knew the three were worthy and formed bonds of friendship with them.
translations.idiomatic: Taigong saw their worth and befriended them.

sentences.zh: 及紂囚文王，乃以黃金千鎰與宜生，令求諸物與紂。

translations.literal: When Zhou imprisoned King Wen, he then gave a thousand yi of gold to Yisheng, ordering him to seek various objects for Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: When King Zhou jailed King Wen, he gave Yisheng a thousand yi of gold to buy curios for the tyrant.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰「閎夭之徒乃求有莘美女，驪戎文馬，有熊九駟，它奇怪物，因殷孽臣費仲獻之於紂，紂大說，乃赦之」也。

translations.literal: ” The Records says “Hong Yao’s party then sought a beautiful woman from Youxin, patterned horses from the Li Rong, nine teams of horses from Youxiong, and other strange things, and through the Yin favorite minister Fei Zhong presented them to Zhou; Zhou was greatly pleased and thereupon pardoned him.”
translations.idiomatic: The Shiji tells how Hong Yao’s party procured beauties, rare horses, and oddities and bribed Fei Zhong until Zhou released Wen.

sentences.zh: 注[七]先賢行狀曰「瑜字雉璜。

translations.literal: Note [7] The Xianxian xingzhuang says “Yu’s courtesy name was Zhihuang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Pei Yu’s courtesy name was Zhihuang.

sentences.zh: 聰明敏達，觀物無滯。

translations.literal: Clever, intelligent, quick and penetrating, observing things without blockage.
translations.idiomatic: He was quick, perceptive, and never baffled by detail.

sentences.zh: 清論所加，必為成器；

translations.literal: Where his clear judgments fell, they were sure to become accomplished instruments;
translations.idiomatic: Those he praised matured into useful men;

sentences.zh: 丑議所指，沒齒無怨」也。

translations.literal: where his blunt criticisms pointed, to the end of their teeth they bore no resentment.”
translations.idiomatic: those he censured swallowed the rebuke without lasting rancor.”

sentences.zh: 論曰：夫剛烈表性，鮮能優寬；

translations.literal: The judgment says: As for men whose nature shows fierce integrity, few can be easy and broad;
translations.idiomatic: The historian observes: stern integrity rarely pairs with easy tolerance;

sentences.zh: 仁柔用情，多乏貞直。

translations.literal: those who use tenderness in human feeling often lack firm uprightness.
translations.idiomatic: gentle temperaments seldom stay straight under pressure.

sentences.zh: 吳季英視人畏傷，發言烝烝，[一]似夫儒者；

translations.literal: Wu Jiying looked on others as if fearing to hurt them; his words came thick and warm, Note [1] like a Ru scholar;
translations.idiomatic: Wu You spoke as if every word might wound—soft as a pedant,

sentences.zh: 而懷憤激揚，折讓權枉，又何壯也！

translations.literal: yet cherishing indignation he rose against crooked power—how heroic!
translations.idiomatic: yet he flung himself against corrupt grandees—how bold!

sentences.zh: 仁以鄉物，義以退身，君子哉！

translations.literal: With benevolence he faced the world, with duty he withdrew from office—a gentleman indeed!
translations.idiomatic: He met the world with kindness and stepped down for principle: a true gentleman.

sentences.zh: [二]語曰：「活千人者子孫必封。

translations.literal: Note [2] The saying goes: “He who saves a thousand men—his descendants will surely be enfeoffed.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The proverb promises noble rank to the house that saves a thousand lives.

sentences.zh: 」[三]史弼頡頏嚴吏，[四]終全平原之黨，而其後不大，[五]斯亦未可論也。

translations.literal: ” Note [3] Shi Bi matched wits with harsh officials, Note [4] and in the end preserved the Pingyuan faction, yet his posterity did not flourish, Note [5]—this too cannot be fully explained.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi defied brutal inspectors and spared a thousand households, yet his line never rose high—fate is not ours to argue.

sentences.zh: 注[一]烝烝猶仍也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Zhengzheng means “still” or “again.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Zhengzheng here means “repeatedly” or “continuously.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]法言曰：「君子於仁也柔，於義也剛。」

translations.literal: Note [2] The Fayan says: “The gentleman is tender in benevolence but firm in duty.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Yang Xiong’s Fayan pairs soft-hearted humanity with hard-edged righteousness.

sentences.zh: 注[三]前書王翁孺曰：「聞活千人者有封*[子]*孫。

translations.literal: Note [3] Wang Wengju of the Former Documents said: “I have heard that he who saves a thousand men will have enfeoffed *[descendants]*.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Wang Zunjun recalled the saying about saving a thousand souls.

sentences.zh: 吾所活者千人，*[後]*世其興乎？」

translations.literal: Those I have saved are a thousand—will *[later]* generations rise?”
translations.idiomatic: “I have spared a thousand lives—will my house rise?”

sentences.zh: 注[四]頡頏猶上下也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Xiehang means up and down, i.e., contending.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Xiehang means to wrestle or contend on equal terms.

sentences.zh: 注[五]不大謂子孫衰替也。

translations.literal: Note [5] “Not great” means descendants declined and faded.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Posterity not great” means his line faded rather than flourished.

sentences.zh: 左傳晉卜偃曰：「畢萬之後必大。」

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition: the Jin diviner Yan said: “The house of Bi Wan will surely grow great.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Zuo Tradition promises greatness to Bi Wan’s descendants—yet fate is uneven.

sentences.zh: 盧植字子幹，涿郡涿人也。

translations.literal: Lu Zhi, courtesy name Zigan, was a man of Zhuo in Zhuo commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhi, courtesy name Zigan, came from Zhuo in Zhuo commandery.

sentences.zh: 身長八尺二寸，音聲如鐘。

translations.literal: His body was eight chi two cun in height; his voice was like a bell.
translations.idiomatic: He stood eight feet two inches tall and boomed like a bronze bell.

sentences.zh: 少與鄭玄俱事馬融，能通古今學，好研精而不守章句。

translations.literal: In youth he studied with Zheng Xuan under Ma Rong, was able to master learning ancient and modern, loved to investigate essentials and did not cling to chapter-and-verse.
translations.idiomatic: He and Zheng Xuan studied under Ma Rong, ranged over classical and current learning, and pursued deep principles instead of literalism.

sentences.zh: 融外戚豪家，[一]多列女倡歌舞於前。

translations.literal: Rong was a powerful house of outer-court kin, Note [1] and often lined up female entertainers to sing and dance before him.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Ma Rong’s clan was wealthy and royal; he kept singing girls in his lecture hall.

sentences.zh: 植侍講積年，未嘗轉眄，融以是敬之。

translations.literal: Zhi attended lectures for years and never shifted his glance; Rong therefore respected him.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhi sat through years of lessons without glancing at the dancers, and Ma Rong honored him for it.

sentences.zh: 學終辭歸，闔門教授。

translations.literal: When study ended he took leave and returned, closing his gate to teach.
translations.idiomatic: When his course finished he went home and taught behind closed doors.

sentences.zh: 性剛毅有大節，常懷濟世志，不好辭賦，能飲酒一石。

translations.literal: By nature he was stern and resolute with great integrity, always cherishing the ambition to aid the age; he did not care for rhapsody, and could drink a dan of wine.
translations.idiomatic: He was austere, ambitious for the realm, cared little for belles-lettres, and could down a full dan of wine.

sentences.zh: 注[一]融，明德皇后之從侄也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Rong was a cousin’s son of Empress Mingde.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Ma Rong was a nephew of Empress Mingde of the Eastern Han.

sentences.zh: 時皇后父大將軍竇武援立靈帝，初秉機政，朝議欲加封爵。

translations.literal: At that time the empress’s father, Grand General Dou Wu, who had set up Emperor Ling, first held the levers of policy, and court discussion wished to add titles of nobility.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Wu, the queen’s father who had enthroned Emperor Ling, now dominated policy, and the court debated lavish enfeoffment.

sentences.zh: 植雖布衣，以武素有名譽，乃獻書以規之曰：「植聞嫠有不恤緯之事，[一]漆室有倚楹之戚，[二] 憂深思遠，君子之情。

translations.literal: Zhi, though in plain clothes, because Wu had long enjoyed a good reputation, therefore submitted a letter to admonish him, saying: “Zhi has heard that widows have the affair of not pitying their weft, Note [1] and the girl of Lacquer Chamber had the grief of leaning on a pillar, Note [2]—deep care and long thought are the feelings of a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Though only a commoner, Lu Zhi wrote Dou Wu a long remonstrance: “I recall the widow who forgot her shuttle from worry for the Zhou house, and the Lacquer-Chamber girl who wept against a pillar—such is a gentleman’s far-sighted care.”

sentences.zh: [三]夫士立爭友，義貴切磋。

translations.literal: Note [3] For a gentleman to keep a disputing friend is right—he values mutual honing.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: A nobleman keeps friends who will argue him sharp.

sentences.zh: [四]書陳『謀及庶人』，[五] 詩詠『詢於芻蕘』。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Documents sets forth “consult the common people,” Note [5] the Poetry sings “ask the woodcutters.”
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu tells kings to ask the people; the Shi jing praises consulting the woodcutter.

sentences.zh: [六]植誦先王之書久矣，敢愛其瞽言哉！

translations.literal: Note [6] Zhi has long recited the writings of the former kings—how dare I spare my blind words!
translations.idiomatic: I have studied the sages too long to hoard blunt advice.

sentences.zh: [七]今足下之於漢朝，猶旦、奭之在周室，建立聖主，四海有系。

translations.literal: Note [7] Now for you, sir, toward the Han court it is as Dan and Shi toward the Zhou house: you established a sage ruler and the four seas have a tether.
translations.idiomatic: You stand to the Han as the Duke of Zhou and the Grand Duke to the young Zhou kings—you enthroned a sage and gave the empire an anchor.

sentences.zh: 論者以為吾子之功，於斯為重。

translations.literal: Debaters think my lord’s merit is heaviest here.
translations.idiomatic: Public opinion calls that your crowning deed.

sentences.zh: 天下聚目而視，攢耳而聽，[八]謂准之前事，將有景風之祚。

translations.literal: The realm gathers eyes to watch and cups ears to listen, Note [8] expecting that measured against precedents there will be blessings like the “bright wind.”
translations.idiomatic: All eyes and ears wait to see whether you will earn the “bright wind” of ancient paragons.

sentences.zh: [九]尋春秋之義，王后無嗣，擇立親長，年均以德，德均則決之卜筮。

translations.literal: Note [9] Searching the meaning of the Spring and Autumn, when the queen has no heir, one selects and establishes the nearest senior by kin; if ages are equal, use virtue; if virtue is equal, decide by tortoise and milfoil.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn rule: without an heir, choose the eldest qualified kinsman; if ages match, weigh virtue; if virtue matches, trust divination.

sentences.zh: [一０]今同宗相後，披圖案牒，以次建之，何勳之有？

translations.literal: Note [10] Now the same surname succeeds in turn; open the charts and registers and establish in order—what special merit is there?
translations.idiomatic: The Liu clan now picks successors by genealogy—where is the unique merit in that?

sentences.zh: 豈橫叨天功以為己力乎！

translations.literal: How can you rashly claim Heaven’s work as your own strength!
translations.idiomatic: How can you pocket Heaven’s credit as private heroism?

sentences.zh: [一一]宜辭大賞，以全身名。

translations.literal: Note [11] You ought to decline the grand reward to preserve body and name.
translations.idiomatic: Refuse the huge reward and keep your life and reputation whole.

sentences.zh: 又比世祚不競，[一二]仍外求嗣，可謂危矣。

translations.literal: Moreover, in recent generations the royal fortune has not been strong, Note [12] yet repeatedly the heir has been sought outside—this may be called perilous.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Lately the dynasty’s qi has faltered while the throne keeps looking outward for heirs—dangerous business.

sentences.zh: 而四方未寧，盜賊伺隙，恆岳、勃碣，[一三]特多奸盜，將有楚人脅比，尹氏立朝之變。

translations.literal: Yet the four quarters are not quiet; bandits watch for openings; Mount Heng and the Bohai coast, Note [13] especially swarm with traitors—there may be changes like the men of Chu forcing alliances or the Yin minister ruling court.
translations.idiomatic: The frontiers seethe with rebels; the Yan–Zhao corridor breeds plotters—think of Chu’s hostage kings or the Duke of Yin’s coup.

sentences.zh: [一四]宜依古禮，置諸子之官，征王侯愛子，宗室賢才，外崇訓道之義，內息貪利之心，簡其良能，隨用爵之，強幹弱枝之道也。

translations.literal: Note [14] You should follow ancient ritual, establish offices for the various sons, summon the beloved sons of kings and marquises and worthy talents of the imperial clan—outwardly to honor the duty of instruction, inwardly to still greedy hearts; select their good capacity and enfeoff them as fit—this is the way to strengthen the trunk and weaken the branches.
translations.idiomatic: Restore the old “sons of the blood” schools, call imperial princes’ heirs and talented kinsmen to the capital for training, and bind them with measured ranks—that is how you thicken the root and thin the twigs.”

sentences.zh: 」[一五] 武並不能用。

translations.literal: ” Note [15] Wu altogether could not use it.
translations.idiomatic: Dou Wu ignored every word.

sentences.zh: 州郡數命，植皆不就。

translations.literal: Commanderies and provinces summoned him repeatedly; Zhi would not go.
translations.idiomatic: Provincial posts came again and again; Lu Zhi refused them all.

sentences.zh: 建寧中，征為博士，乃始起焉。

translations.literal: In the Jianning era he was summoned as erudite, and only then did he first rise to office.
translations.idiomatic: During Jianning the court finally called him up as a doctoral scholar.

sentences.zh: 熹平四年，九江蠻反，四府選植才兼文武，拜九江太守，蠻寇賓服。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Xiping the Jiujiang Man rebelled; the four high bureaus chose Zhi for talent in both civil and military affairs, appointed him Administrator of Jiujiang, and the Man raiders submitted.
translations.idiomatic: In 175 the Jiujiang tribes rose; the high ministers sent Lu Zhi as governor, and he pacified them by arms and policy.

sentences.zh: 以疾去官。

translations.literal: He left office because of illness.
translations.idiomatic: Ill health forced him to resign.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳曰，范獻子曰：「人亦有言，嫠不恤其緯而憂宗周之隕，為將及焉。

translations.literal: Note [1] The Zuo Tradition: Fan Xianzi said: “There is a common saying: the widow does not pity her weft but grieves the fall of the Zhou house, for disaster will reach her.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Fan Xianzi’s Zuo Tradition tag tells of a widow who forgot her loom from worry for the realm.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注曰：「嫠，寡婦也。

translations.literal: ” Du Yu’s commentary says: “Li means a widow.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses li as “widow.”

sentences.zh: 織者常苦緯少，寡婦所宜憂也。」

translations.literal: Weavers always suffer from too little weft—it is what a widow ought to worry about.”
translations.idiomatic: A weaver’s petty trouble is short weft—but a widow should fear the state’s ruin.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]琴操曰：「魯漆室女倚柱悲吟而嘯，□人見其心之不樂也，進而問之曰：

translations.literal: Note [2] The Qincao says: “The girl of Lu’s Lacquer Chamber leaned on a pillar, sadly chanting and whistling; □ people saw her heart was not happy and stepped forward asking:
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Qincao tells of a Lu girl who moaned against a pillar until neighbors asked,

sentences.zh: 『有淫心欲嫁之念耶，何吟之悲？

translations.literal: “Do you harbor a lustful heart wishing to marry—why is your chant so sad?”
translations.idiomatic: “Are you pining for a husband? Why such grief?”

sentences.zh: 』漆室女曰：『嗟乎！

translations.literal: ” The Lacquer-Chamber girl said: “Alas!
translations.idiomatic: She cried, “Alas!

sentences.zh: 嗟乎！

translations.literal: Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Alas!

sentences.zh: 子無志，不知人之甚也。

translations.literal: You have no ambition—you do not know people at all.
translations.idiomatic: You have no sense—you read a human heart so shallowly.

sentences.zh: 昔者楚人得罪於其君，走逃吾東家，馬逸，蹈吾園葵，使吾終年不□菜；

translations.literal: Once a man of Chu offended his lord and fled to my east neighbor; his horse bolted and trampled my garden mallow, so I could not gather mallows all year;
translations.idiomatic: When a Chu fugitive’s horse ruined my greens I went hungry a year;

sentences.zh: 吾西□人失羊不還，請吾兄追之，霧濁水出，使吾兄溺死，終身無兄。

translations.literal: my west □ neighbor lost sheep and did not return, asked my elder brother to pursue them, mist and turbid water rose, and my elder brother drowned—so I have no elder brother for life.
translations.idiomatic: when a western neighbor lost sheep my brother drowned chasing it through flood—I am brotherless forever.

sentences.zh: 政之所致也。

translations.literal: That is what government brought about.
translations.idiomatic: Misrule did that to me.”

sentences.zh: 吾憂國傷人，心悲而嘯，豈欲嫁哉！

translations.literal: I grieve for the state and pity people; my heart is sad and I whistle—how could I wish to marry!”
translations.idiomatic: “I whistle from sorrow for the realm, not from desire for a match!”

sentences.zh: 』自傷懷結而為人所疑，於是褰裳入山林之中，見女貞之木，喟然歎息，援琴而弦歌以女貞之辭，自經而死。」

translations.literal: ” She wounded herself with pent feelings and was suspected by others; then she hitched her skirt and entered the mountain forest, saw the female-virtue tree, sighed deeply, took up a zither and sang to the tune of “Female Virtue,” and hanged herself.”
translations.idiomatic: Heartbroken and misunderstood, she fled to the woods, saw the chaste-tree, played the “Chaste Wife” song, and hanged herself.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]詩序曰：「憂深思遠，儉而用禮，乃有堯之遺風焉。」

translations.literal: Note [3] The Preface to the Poetry says: “Deep care and long thought, frugal yet using ritual—therein lies the surviving wind of Yao.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Mao preface praises states whose air is frugal, ritual-minded, and far-sighted like Yao’s.

sentences.zh: 注[四]孝經曰：「士有爭友，身不陷於不義。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Classic of Filial Piety says: “The gentleman has a disputing friend, so his person does not sink into unrighteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Xiaojing says a disputing friend keeps one from wrong.

sentences.zh: 」詩云：「如切如磋。

translations.literal: ” The Poetry says: “As cutting, as polishing.”
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Poetry compares friends to bone and ivory worked smooth.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注云：「骨曰切，像曰磋。

translations.literal: ” Zheng Xuan’s commentary says: “Bone is called cutting; tusk is called polishing.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses “cutting” and “polishing” as working bone and horn.

sentences.zh: 言友之相規誡，如骨象之見切磋。」

translations.literal: It means that friends admonish one another as bone and tusk appear under cutting and polishing.”
translations.idiomatic: Friends sharpen one another the same way.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]尚書洪範曰「謀及卿士，謀及庶人」也。

translations.literal: Note [5] The Documents, “Great Plan,” says “consult the high ministers, consult the common people.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Hong fan counsels consulting ministers and commoners alike.

sentences.zh: 注[六]詩大雅曰：「先人有言，詢於芻蕘。

translations.literal: Note [6] A Greater Ya ode says: “The ancients had a saying—ask the woodcutter.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Greater Ya urges rulers to ask even the fuel-gatherers.

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「芻蕘，采薪者也。」

translations.literal: ” Mao Chang’s commentary says: “Chu rao means those who gather firewood.”
translations.idiomatic: Mao Chang defines chu rao as men who cut kindling.

sentences.zh: 注[七]無目□曰瞽。

translations.literal: Note [7] Without eyes □ is called gu (blind).
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gu means blind, lacking sight.

sentences.zh: □音直忍反。

translations.literal: □ is read zhi ren fan.
translations.idiomatic: The graph is read with the fanqie spelling zhi-ren.

sentences.zh: 注[八]前書賈山曰「使天下戴目而視，傾耳而聽」也。

translations.literal: Note [8] Jia Shan of the Former Documents said “make the realm cap its eyes to watch and cup its ears to listen.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Jia Shan’s phrase describes all China watching and listening.

sentences.zh: 注[九]景風，解見和紀。

translations.literal: Note [9] “Bright wind”—the explanation appears in the annals of harmony.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Bright wind” is glossed in the reign-treatise on harmony.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]左傳王子朝曰：「先王之命，王后無嫡，則擇立長。

translations.literal: Note [10] The Zuo Tradition, Prince Zhao of Zhou, says: “By the command of the former kings, when the queen has no eldest son, then choose and establish the eldest.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Prince Zhao’s speech sets the rule for choosing an heir when the queen has no firstborn.

sentences.zh: 年鈞以德，德鈞以卜，古之制也。」

translations.literal: If years are equal, use virtue; if virtue is equal, use tortoise and milfoil—such was the ancient system.”
translations.idiomatic: Age yields to virtue, virtue to divination—so ran the old law.”

sentences.zh: 注[一一]叨，貪也。

translations.literal: Note [11] Tao means greed.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Tao means to covet or steal credit.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「貪天之功，以為己力」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says “greed for Heaven’s achievement, taking it as one’s own strength.”
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition condemns “stealing Heaven’s merit as one’s own.”

sentences.zh: 注[一二]競，強也。

translations.literal: Note [12] Jing means strong.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Jing here means vigorous or robust.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]勃，勃海也。

translations.literal: Note [13] Bo means Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Bo” is Bohai sea.

sentences.zh: 碣，碣石山也。

translations.literal: Jie means Mount Jieshi.
translations.idiomatic: “Jie” is Mount Jieshi on the coast.

sentences.zh: 注[一四]左傳曰，楚公子比，恭王之子也。

translations.literal: Note [14] The Zuo Tradition: Prince Bi of Chu was a son of King Gong.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Prince Bi of Chu was King Gong’s son.

sentences.zh: 靈王立，子比奔晉。

translations.literal: When King Ling was established, Prince Bi fled to Jin.
translations.idiomatic: When King Ling took the throne, Bi fled to Jin.

sentences.zh: 靈王卒，子比自晉歸楚，立為君。

translations.literal: When King Ling died, Prince Bi returned from Jin to Chu and was set up as lord.
translations.idiomatic: On Ling’s death Bi returned from Jin and was enthroned.

sentences.zh: 比弟公子□疾欲篡其位，夜乃使人周走呼曰：「王至矣。

translations.literal: Bi’s younger brother Prince □ Ji wished to usurp his place, and at night had men run about shouting: “The king has arrived!
translations.idiomatic: His brother Ji plotted a coup and had runners cry at night, “The king is coming!”

sentences.zh: 」國人大驚，子比乃自殺。

translations.literal: ” The great people were greatly alarmed, and Prince Bi then killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: The capital panicked and Bi killed himself.

sentences.zh: 王子朝，周景王之庶子。

translations.literal: Prince Zhao of Zhou was a younger son of King Jing of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Zhao was a bastard son of King Jing of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 景王卒，子猛立。

translations.literal: When King Jing died, the son Meng was set up.
translations.idiomatic: When Jing died, Crown Prince Meng briefly ruled.

sentences.zh: 尹氏，周卿士，立子朝，奪猛位也。

translations.literal: The Yin clan, senior minister of Zhou, set up Prince Zhao and seized Meng’s place.
translations.idiomatic: Minister Yin of Zhou enthroned Zhao and ousted Meng.

sentences.zh: 注[一五]以樹為喻也。

translations.literal: Note [15] It uses the tree as metaphor.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The “trunk and branch” image explains strengthening the capital.

sentences.zh: 謂京師為干，四方為枝。

translations.literal: It means taking the capital region as the trunk and the four quarters as branches.
translations.idiomatic: The capital is the trunk; the commanderies are the limbs.

sentences.zh: 前書曰：「漢興，立都長安，徙齊諸田、楚昭、屈、景及諸功臣家於長陵。

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: “When Han rose it set its capital at Chang’an and moved the Tian of Qi, the Zhao, Qu, and Jing of Chu, and the households of various merit ministers to Changling.
translations.idiomatic: The Hanshu records how Gaozu planted powerful clans around Chang’an to guard the core.

sentences.zh: 蓋以強幹弱枝，非獨為奉山園也。」

translations.literal: This was in order to strengthen the trunk and weaken the branches—not only to serve the imperial tombs.”
translations.idiomatic: The policy was to thicken the root, not merely to populate the mausoleum towns.”

sentences.zh: 作尚書章句、三禮解詁。

translations.literal: He composed chapter-and-verse glosses on the Documents and exegeses on the three ritual classics.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote a segmented commentary on the Shang shu and glosses on the three ritual books.

sentences.zh: [一]時始立太學石經，以正五經文字，植乃上書曰：「臣少從通儒故南郡太守馬融受古學，頗知今之禮記特多回□。

translations.literal: Note [1] At that time they first set up the stone classics at the Imperial Academy to correct the graphs of the five classics; Zhi therefore submitted a memorial saying: “Your servant in youth followed the comprehensive scholar, former Administrator of Nan Commandery Ma Rong, in ancient learning, and knows rather well that today’s Record of Rites contains many twists □.
translations.idiomatic: When the stone classics went up at the academy, Lu Zhi wrote that he had studied under Ma Rong and found the current Liji full of corrupt passages.

sentences.zh: [二]臣前以周禮諸經，發起□謬，[三]敢率愚淺，為之解詁，而家乏，無力供繕*[寫]*上。

translations.literal: Note [2] Your servant formerly, using the Rites of Zhou and other classics, exposed □ errors, Note [3] and dares in his folly to have produced exegeses, but the household is poor and lacks strength to supply a fair copy *[to present]* upward.
translations.idiomatic: He had already drafted corrections but was too poor to copy them out for the throne.

sentences.zh: [四] 願得將書生二人，共詣東觀，就官財糧，專心研精，合尚書章句，考禮記失得，庶裁定聖典，刊正碑文。

translations.literal: Note [4] I beg to take two students and together go to the Eastern Pavilion, relying on official funds and grain, concentrating mind to refine them, combine the chapter-and-verse gloss on the Documents, examine the gains and losses of the Record of Rites—perhaps thereby to settle the sage canon and correct the stone inscription text.
translations.idiomatic: He asked for two copyists, state rations at the Eastern Pavilion, and time to merge his Shang shu gloss with a critical edition of the Liji for the steles.

sentences.zh: 古文科斗，近於為實，而厭抑流俗，降在小學。

translations.literal: Ancient script and tadpole script are close to being authentic, yet are suppressed by vulgar fashion and relegated to “elementary studies.”
translations.idiomatic: Old-wall script and seal forms are truest to antiquity yet dismissed as “minor learning.”

sentences.zh: [五] 中興以來，通儒達士班固、賈逵、鄭興父子，並敦悅之。

translations.literal: Note [5] Since the restoration, comprehensive scholars such as Ban Gu, Jia Kui, and Zheng Xing and his son have all delighted in them.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Eastern Han scholars from Ban Gu to Zheng Xing cherished those scripts.

sentences.zh: [六]今毛詩、左氏、周禮各有傳記，其興春秋共相表裡，[七]宜置博士，為立學官，以助後來，以廣聖意。

translations.literal: Note [6] Now the Mao Odes, Zuo Tradition, and Rites of Zhou each have commentaries; they together support the Spring and Autumn, Note [7]—it is fitting to appoint erudites and establish academic officers to help those who come later and broaden the sage’s intent.
translations.idiomatic: He urged doctoral chairs for Mao, Zuo, and Zhou li to anchor the Spring and Autumn curriculum.

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]詁，事也。

translations.literal: ” Note [1] Gu means “affair.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gu means “matter” or “subject.”

sentences.zh: 言解其事意。

translations.literal: It means explaining the meaning of the affair.
translations.idiomatic: That is, to unpack the sense.

sentences.zh: 注[二]回□猶紆曲也。

translations.literal: Note [2] Hui □ means tortuous.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The phrase means convoluted or crooked wording.

sentences.zh: 注[三]□，粟不成。

translations.literal: Note [3] □ means grain not formed.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The graph names shriveled grain—

sentences.zh: 諭義之乖僻也。

translations.literal: It illustrates meanings that are perverse.
translations.idiomatic: —a metaphor for twisted doctrine.

sentences.zh: 注[四]繕，善也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Shan means “good.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Shan here means a clean, fair copy.

sentences.zh: 言家貧不能善寫而上也。

translations.literal: It means the household is poor and cannot copy well to present upward.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhi means he cannot afford a fair manuscript for the palace.

sentences.zh: 注[五]古文謂孔子壁中書也。

translations.literal: Note [5] “Ancient script” means the texts from Confucius’ wall.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Ancient script” denotes the wall manuscripts.

sentences.zh: 形似科斗，因以為名。

translations.literal: Their shapes resemble tadpoles, hence the name.
translations.idiomatic: Their strokes look like tadpoles, hence the nickname.

sentences.zh: 前書謂文字為「小學」也。

translations.literal: The Former Documents calls writing studies “elementary learning.”
translations.idiomatic: The Hanshu classed paleography as “elementary learning.”

sentences.zh: 注[六]興子觿也，自有傳。

translations.literal: Note [6] Xing’s son is Xi; he has his own commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Zheng Xing’s son Zheng Xi wrote independent glosses.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰「□縠悅禮樂而敦詩書」也。

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says “□ Gu delighted in ritual and music and honored the Documents and Poetry.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Zuo praises a minister who loved ritual, music, and the classics.

sentences.zh: 注[七]表裡言義相須而成也。

translations.literal: Note [7] “Outer and inner” means the meanings need each other to be complete.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Mutual outer and inner” means the texts illuminate one another.

sentences.zh: 前書云：「河圖、洛書相為經緯，八卦、九章相為表裡。」

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: “The River chart and Luo writing are warp and woof to each other; the eight trigrams and nine chapters are outer and inner to each other.”
translations.idiomatic: The Hanshu pairs charts and documents as warp and woof.

sentences.zh: 會南夷反叛，以植嘗在九江有恩信，拜為廬江太守。

translations.literal: It happened that the southern barbarians rebelled; because Zhi had once had kindness and trust in Jiujiang, he was appointed Administrator of Lujiang.
translations.idiomatic: When southern tribes rose, the court sent Lu Zhi to Lujiang, where his old kindness from Jiujiang still won trust.

sentences.zh: 植深達政宜，務存清靜，弘大體而已。

translations.literal: Zhi deeply grasped what was fitting in government; he strove to keep things clear and quiet, enlarging the great pattern and that was all.
translations.idiomatic: He governed Lujiang with quiet breadth—few measures, large principle.

sentences.zh: [一一]帝不省。

translations.literal: Note [11] The emperor did not examine.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Emperor Ling ignored the advice (or did not look into the matter).

sentences.zh: 注[一]言中書以別於外也。

translations.literal: Note [1] It speaks of the Secretariat to distinguish it from the outer court.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Inner” here means the palace secretariat.

sentences.zh: 注[二]五行傳，劉向所著。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Wuxing zhuan was composed by Liu Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Liu Xiang’s treatise on the five phases.

sentences.zh: 朓者，月行速在日前，故早見。

translations.literal: Tiao means the moon runs fast before the sun, so it appears early.
translations.idiomatic: A “hasty” moon outruns the sun and shows too soon.

sentences.zh: 劉向以為君舒緩則臣*

translations.literal: Liu Xiang took it that when the ruler is lax then ministers*
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang read it as an omen of slack rule—ministers—

translations.literal: (jiao)
translations.idiomatic: The marginal gloss gives an alternate graph for “arrogant.”

sentences.zh: **[驕]*慢，故日行□而月行速也。

translations.literal: **[jiao]* grow arrogant, therefore the sun’s course □ while the moon’s course is fast.
translations.idiomatic: —grow arrogant, so the sun seems slow and the moon races ahead.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左氏傳曰：「日過分未至三辰有□，於是乎君不舉，避移時。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Zuo Tradition says: “When the sun passes the equinox and has not yet reached the third mark there is □, thereupon the ruler does not hold a feast and avoids for a shifted hour.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Zuo prescribes abstaining from court meals during certain solar omens.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注曰：「避正寑，過日食時也。」

translations.literal: ” Du Yu’s commentary says: “He avoids the main bedchamber until the hour of the eclipse passes.”
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu says the ruler leaves the main hall until the eclipse ends.

sentences.zh: 注[四]原其所禁而宥之也。

translations.literal: Note [4] It means investigating what is forbidden and pardoning it.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Investigate the taboo, then grant amnesty.

sentences.zh: 注[五]防禦疫癘之氣。

translations.literal: Note [5] To guard against pestilential vapors.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Rites to ward off epidemic miasma.

sentences.zh: 注[六]核，實也。

translations.literal: Note [6] He means “kernel” or “fact.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: He means solid fact or pit.

sentences.zh: 注[七]回，邪也。

translations.literal: Note [7] Hui means deviant.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Hui means crooked or heterodox.

sentences.zh: 注[八]後以王甫、程阿所構，憂死，父及兄弟並被誅。

translations.literal: Note [8] Later, through slander by Wang Fu and Cheng A, he died of grief; his father and brothers were all executed.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Slander by Wang Fu and Cheng A drove him to grief-death; his kin were executed.

sentences.zh: 靈帝后夢見桓帝怒曰「宋皇後何罪而絕其命？

translations.literal: Emperor Ling’s empress later dreamed that Emperor Huan angrily said “What crime had Empress Song that you cut off her life?
translations.idiomatic: Empress Song appeared to Emperor Ling’s consort in a dream: Huan’s ghost demanded why Song was murdered.

sentences.zh: 已訴於天，上帝震怒，罪在難救」也。

translations.literal: She has already complained to Heaven; the High Lord is enraged—the guilt is beyond saving.”
translations.idiomatic: “She has appealed to Heaven; the sentence cannot be lifted.”

sentences.zh: 注[九]書曰：「三載考績，黜陟幽明。

translations.literal: Note [9] The Documents says: “Every three years examine merit, demote the dim and promote the bright.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Shang shu mandates triennial review of officials.

sentences.zh: 」孔安國注曰：「三年考功，三考九年，能否幽明有別，升進其明者，黜退其幽者。

translations.literal: ” Kong Anguo’s commentary says: “Every three years examine work; thrice examined makes nine years—then able and unable, dim and bright are distinguished; promote the bright, demote the dim.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo explains nine-year cycles of promotion and demotion.

sentences.zh: 」此皆唐堯之法也。

translations.literal: ” These are all methods of Tang Yao.”
translations.idiomatic: These were Tang Yao’s methods of governance.”

sentences.zh: 注[一０]希，求也。

translations.literal: Note [10] Xi means to seek.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Xi means to seek or importune.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]蠲，除也。

translations.literal: Note [11] Juan means to remove.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Juan means to remit or cancel.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，黃巾賊起，四府舉植，拜北中郎將，持節，以護烏桓中郎將宗員副，將北軍五校士，發天下諸郡兵征之。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhongping the Yellow Turban bandits rose; the four high bureaus recommended Zhi, appointed him Colonel of the Northern Army with credentials, made Colonel of the Wuhuan Zhong Lang Jiang Zong Yuan his deputy, led the five northern camp corps, and issued troops from all commanderies to campaign against them.
translations.idiomatic: In 184 the Yellow Turbans erupted; Lu Zhi was named North Colonel with banner authority, Zong Yuan as Wuhuan colonel seconded him, and they led imperial troops from every circuit.

sentences.zh: 連戰破賊帥張角，斬獲萬餘人。

translations.literal: In successive battles he defeated the bandit leader Zhang Jiao, beheading and capturing more than ten thousand.
translations.idiomatic: He smashed Zhang Jiao’s host and took over ten thousand heads and prisoners.

sentences.zh: 角等走保廣宗，植築圍鑿燎，造作雲梯，垂當拔之。

translations.literal: Jiao and others fled to hold Guangzong; Zhi built siegeworks, dug moats with fire-pits, made cloud-ladders, and was on the point of taking it.
translations.idiomatic: The rebels barricaded Guangzong; Lu Zhi ringed the city with ditches, fires, and ladders and was ready to storm it.

sentences.zh: 帝遣小黃門左豐詣軍觀賊形埶，或勸植以賂送豐，植不肯。

translations.literal: The emperor sent Junior Attendant of the Yellow Gate Zuo Feng to the army to observe the bandits’ situation; some urged Zhi to bribe Feng, but Zhi would not.
translations.idiomatic: A court eunuch came to spy on the siege; aides urged Lu Zhi to bribe him—he refused.

sentences.zh: 豐還言於帝曰：「廣宗賊易破耳。

translations.literal: Feng returned and said to the emperor: “The Guangzong bandits are easy to break.
translations.idiomatic: Zuo Feng told the throne the rebels were easy meat.

sentences.zh: 盧中郎固壘息軍，以待天誅。

translations.literal: Colonel Lu solidifies his ramparts and rests his army, waiting for Heaven’s punishment.”
translations.idiomatic: “Yet Colonel Lu sits behind walls, idle, waiting for Heaven to strike them.”

sentences.zh: 」帝怒，遂檻車征植，減死罪一等。

translations.literal: ” The emperor was angry and therefore summoned Zhi in a caged cart, reducing the death penalty by one degree.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling raged, recalled Lu Zhi in fetters, and spared his life only by one degree.

sentences.zh: 及車騎將車皇甫嵩討平黃巾，盛稱植行師方略，嵩皆資用規謀，濟成其功。

translations.literal: When Grand Commandant of Chariots and Cavalry Huangfu Song campaigned to pacify the Yellow Turbans, he greatly praised Zhi’s methods in marching armies; Song in all things drew on his plans and counsels, and thereby accomplished the merit.
translations.idiomatic: When Huangfu Song finished the war, he told the court that Lu Zhi’s strategy had made victory possible.

sentences.zh: 以其年復為尚書。

translations.literal: That same year he was again made Minister.
translations.idiomatic: The same year he was restored as Minister.

sentences.zh: 帝崩，大將軍何進謀誅中官，乃召并州牧董卓，以懼太后。

translations.literal: When the emperor died, Grand General He Jin plotted to kill the palace attendants and therefore summoned Bingzhou Governor Dong Zhuo to frighten the empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: At Ling’s death He Jin summoned Dong Zhuo from Bingzhou to cow the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 植知卓凶悍難制，必生後患，固止之。

translations.literal: Zhi knew Zhuo was fierce and hard to control and would surely breed later trouble, and firmly stopped him.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhi warned that Dong Zhuo could not be tamed and would bring disaster.

sentences.zh: 進不從。

translations.literal: Jin would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: He Jin refused to listen.

sentences.zh: 及卓至，果陵虐朝廷，乃大會百官於朝堂，議欲廢立。

translations.literal: When Zhuo arrived, he indeed brutalized the court; then he held a great assembly of the hundred officials in the court hall to discuss deposing and enthroning.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo seized Luoyang, terrorized the court, and called a full assembly to debate deposing the emperor.

sentences.zh: 腢僚無敢言，植獨抗議不同。

translations.literal: Among the assembled officials none dared speak; only Zhi alone protested and disagreed.
translations.idiomatic: The whole bureaucracy held its tongue; only Lu Zhi defied the tyrant’s plan.

sentences.zh: 卓怒罷會，將誅植，語在卓傳。

translations.literal: Zhuo angrily broke up the meeting and was about to kill Zhi—the words are in Zhuo’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo broke up the session and meant to kill him—the full story is in Dong Zhuo’s memoir.

sentences.zh: 植素善蔡邕，邕前徙朔方，植獨上書請之。

translations.literal: Zhi had long been friendly with Cai Yong; when Yong had earlier been banished to Shuofang, Zhi alone submitted a memorial asking for him.
translations.idiomatic: When Cai Yong was sent to Shuofang, only Lu Zhi petitioned for mercy.

sentences.zh: 邕時見親於卓，故往請植事。

translations.literal: Yong at that time was intimate with Zhuo, and therefore went to plead Zhi’s affair.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong, now favored by Dong Zhuo, interceded in turn.

sentences.zh: 又議郎彭伯諫卓曰：「盧尚書海內大儒，人之望也。

translations.literal: Also Gentleman Consultant Peng Bo remonstrated with Zhuo, saying: “Minister Lu is the great Ru of the realm, the hope of the people.
translations.idiomatic: Peng Bo told Dong Zhuo, “Lu Zhi is the empire’s leading scholar—the people look to him.

sentences.zh: 今先害*[之]*，天下震怖。

translations.literal: Now if you first harm *[him]*, the realm will tremble in terror.
translations.idiomatic: Kill him first and the world will panic.”

sentences.zh: 」卓乃止，但免植官而已。

translations.literal: ” Zhuo then stopped, only removing Zhi from office.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo stayed his hand and merely stripped Lu Zhi of his post.

sentences.zh: 植以老病求歸，懼不免禍，乃詭道從轘轅出。

translations.literal: Zhi asked to return home on account of old age and illness, fearing he could not escape disaster, and therefore took byways out through Huanyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhi begged leave on grounds of age and illness, then slipped away by back roads through Huanyuan pass.

sentences.zh: [一]卓果使人追之，到懷，不及。

translations.literal: Note [1] Zhuo indeed sent men in pursuit; they reached Huai but did not catch him.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo’s riders reached Huai but missed him.

sentences.zh: 遂隱於上谷，不交人事。

translations.literal: Thereupon he hid in Shanggu and did not mingle in human affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He hid in Shanggu and shunned the world.

sentences.zh: 冀州牧袁紹請為軍師。

translations.literal: Yuan Shao, Governor of Jizhou, invited him to be army director.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao, governor of Ji province, called him in as military counselor.

sentences.zh: 初平三年卒。

translations.literal: He died in the third year of Chuping.
translations.idiomatic: He died in 192, the third year of Chuping.

sentences.zh: 臨困，□其子儉葬於土穴，不用棺幟，附體單帛而已。

translations.literal: When near death he ordered his son Jian to bury him in an earthen pit, not using coffin or banners, only a single layer of cloth on the body.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed he told his son Lu Jian to lay him in a soil vault with no coffin—only a winding-sheet against his skin.

sentences.zh: 所著碑、誄、表、記凡六篇。

translations.literal: What he wrote—stele texts, dirges, memorials, and records—comes to six pieces in all.
translations.idiomatic: Six of his writings survive: stele inscriptions, dirges, petitions, and notes.

sentences.zh: 注[一]詭，詐也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Gui means deceit.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Gui means cunning or false pretense.

sentences.zh: 轘轅道在今洛州緱氏縣東南也。

translations.literal: The Huanyuan road lies southeast of Gongshi county in Luozhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Huanyuan pass road ran southeast of present Gongshi in Henan.

sentences.zh: 建安中，曹操北討柳城，過涿郡，[一]告守令曰：「故北中郎將盧植，名著海內，學為儒宗，士之楷模，國之楨干也。

translations.literal: In the Jian’an era Cao Cao campaigned north against Liucheng, passed through Zhuo commandery, Note [1] and told the guardian magistrate: “The late Colonel of the Northern Army Lu Zhi—his name was famed within the seas, his learning was patriarch of the Ru, model for gentlemen, pillar of the state.
translations.idiomatic: On his northern campaign in 207 Cao Cao stopped at Zhuo and ordered the local magistrate to honor the late Lu Zhi as the age’s leading scholar and pillar of state.

sentences.zh: 昔武王入殷，封商容之閭；

translations.literal: In olden days King Wu entered Yin and enfeoffed Shang Rong’s lane;
translations.idiomatic: King Wu of Zhou ennobled Shang Rong’s lane when he entered Yin.

sentences.zh: 鄭喪子產，仲尼隕涕。

translations.literal: When Zheng lost Zichan, Zhongni shed tears.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius wept when Zichan died in Zheng.

sentences.zh: [二]孤到此州，嘉其餘風。

translations.literal: Note [2] I, alone, have come to this commandery and admire the lingering wind.
translations.idiomatic: I have come to your circuit and admire what still lingers of his virtue.

sentences.zh: 春秋之義，賢者之後，宜有殊禮。

translations.literal: By the meaning of the Spring and Autumn, the posterity of the worthy ought to receive special rites.
translations.idiomatic: The Chunqiu principle demands special honor for a worthy man’s heirs.

sentences.zh: [三]亟遣丞掾除其墳墓，[四]存其子孫，並致薄醊，[五]以彰厥德。

translations.literal: Note [3] Hasten to send assistant clerks to sweep his tomb, Note [4] support his descendants, and together offer a modest libation, Note [5] to display his virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Send men at once to tend his grave, succor his line, and pour a modest libation in his memory.

sentences.zh: 」子毓，知名。

translations.literal: ” His son Yu became renowned.
translations.idiomatic: His son Lu Yu won fame in his own right.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]魏志曰，建安十二年，操北征烏桓，涉鮮卑，討柳城，登白狼山也。

translations.literal: Note [6] Note [1] The Wei zhi says: in the twelfth year of Jian’an, Cao campaigned north against the Wuhuan, crossed the Xianbei, attacked Liucheng, and ascended Mount Bailang.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Wei zhi dates Cao’s 207 raid on Wuhuan and the climb of Mount Bailang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]左傳曰：「仲尼聞子產死，出涕曰：『古之遺愛也。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Zuo Tradition says: “When Zhongni heard that Zichan had died, he went out weeping and said: ‘He was the surviving love of antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius called Zichan “the love of antiquity” left to the world.

sentences.zh: 』」注[三]公羊傳曰：「君子之善善也長，惡惡也短。

translations.literal: ”” Note [3] The Gongyang Tradition says: “The gentleman’s praising good is long; his hating evil is short.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Gongyang says a gentleman’s praise endures longer than his blame.

sentences.zh: 惡惡止其身，善善及子孫。

translations.literal: Hating evil stops with the person; praising good reaches descendants.
translations.idiomatic: Blame stops with the guilty; praise extends to their children.

sentences.zh: 賢者子孫，故君子為之諱也。」

translations.literal: The descendants of the worthy—therefore the gentleman makes excuse for them.”
translations.idiomatic: Hence the gentleman softens judgment on a sage’s posterity.”

sentences.zh: 注[四]亟，急也。

translations.literal: Note [4] Ji means urgent.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Ji means “at once” or “urgently.”

sentences.zh: 注[五]醊，祭酹也。

translations.literal: Note [5] Zhui means the libation poured in sacrifice.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Zhui is the poured offering at a tomb sacrifice.

sentences.zh: 音張芮反。

translations.literal: It is read zhang rui fan.
translations.idiomatic: The fanqie spelling is zhang-rui.

sentences.zh: 注[六]魏志曰：「毓字子家，十歲而孤，以學行稱，仕魏至侍中、吏部尚書。

translations.literal: Note [6] The Wei zhi says: “Yu’s courtesy name was Zijia; at ten he was orphaned, was praised for learning and conduct, served Wei to palace attendant and Minister of the Ministry of Personnel.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Yu rose in Wei to Minister of Personnel and palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 時舉中書郎，詔曰：『得其人與不，在盧生耳。

translations.literal: When they were selecting a secretary of the Secretariat, an edict said: “Whether the right man is found lies with Master Lu.
translations.idiomatic: An edict once said, “Whether we get the right man for the secretariat rests with Master Lu.”

sentences.zh: 選舉莫取有名，如畫地為餅，不可啖也。

translations.literal: In appointments do not take mere reputation—it is like drawing a cake on the ground; you cannot eat it.”
translations.idiomatic: “Do not pick names off rumor—that is like drawing a cake in the dust.”

sentences.zh: 』毓對曰：『名不足以致異人，而可以得常士。

translations.literal: ” Yu answered: “Reputation is not enough to obtain extraordinary men, but it can obtain ordinary gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Yu replied that fame cannot lure geniuses but can find solid middling talent.

sentences.zh: 常士畏教慕善，然後有名也。

translations.literal: Ordinary gentlemen fear instruction and admire the good—only afterward do they have a name.”
translations.idiomatic: Ordinary men learn to fear teaching and love goodness—then they earn a name.”

sentences.zh: 』」論曰：風霜以別草木之性，[一]危亂而見貞良之節，[二]則盧公之心可知矣。

translations.literal: ”” The judgment says: Wind and frost distinguish the nature of plants and trees, Note [1] peril and disorder reveal the integrity of the loyal and good, Note [2]—then the heart of Lord Lu may be known.
translations.idiomatic: The historian concludes: trial by frost shows pine and oak; trial by chaos shows Lu Zhi’s mettle.

sentences.zh: 夫螽蠆起懷，雷霆駭耳，雖賁、育、荊、諸之倫，[三]未有不冘豫奪常者也。

translations.literal: For when locusts and scorpions rise in the breast and thunderbolts startle the ear—even men of the class of Ben, Yu, Jing, and Zhu, Note [3] there is none who does not falter and lose his composure.
translations.idiomatic: When panic claws the heart and thunder splits the ear, even heroes like Meng Ben falter.

sentences.zh: [四] 當植抽白刃嚴合之下，追帝河津之閒，排戈刃，赴戕折，[五]豈先計哉？

translations.literal: Note [4] Yet when Zhi drew the white blade beneath a stern session, pursued the emperor between river and ford, thrust aside spear-points and went to sudden death, Note [5]—could he have calculated beforehand?
translations.idiomatic: Yet Lu Zhi bared steel under Dong Zhuo’s glare and defied him at the Yellow River—no cold calculation there.

sentences.zh: 君子之於忠義，造次必於是，顛沛必於是也。

translations.literal: For the gentleman toward loyalty and duty—in haste it must be so, in ruin it must be so.
translations.idiomatic: A gentleman keeps faith in the flash of crisis and in the crush of disaster.

sentences.zh: [六] 注[一]論語曰：「歲寒然後知松柏之後雕也。」

translations.literal: Note [6] Note [1] The Analects says: “Only when the year turns cold do we know that pine and cypress are the last to fade.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Analects compares constancy to winter pines.

sentences.zh: 注[二]老子曰：「國家昏亂有忠臣。」

translations.literal: Note [2] Laozi says: “When the state is darkened by chaos, loyal ministers appear.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Laozi says loyal ministers emerge when the kingdom totters.

sentences.zh: 注[三]孟賁，多力者也；

translations.literal: Note [3] Meng Ben was a man of great strength;
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Meng Ben was a famous strongman;

sentences.zh: 夏育，勇者也：並□人。

translations.literal: Xia Yu was a man of courage—both were men of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Xia Yu a famous brave—both hailed from Qi.

sentences.zh: 荊，荊軻也。

translations.literal: Jing is Jing Ke.
translations.idiomatic: “Jing” is the assassin Jing Ke.

sentences.zh: 諸，專諸也。

translations.literal: Zhu is Zhuan Zhu.
translations.idiomatic: “Zhu” is the assassin Zhuan Zhu.

sentences.zh: 注[四]冘，人行貌也，音淫。

translations.literal: Note [4] Yin is the appearance of a man walking, read yin.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Yin-yu describes hesitant pacing.

sentences.zh: 言冘豫不能自定也。

translations.literal: It means yin-yu cannot settle itself.
translations.idiomatic: It means wavering, unable to decide.

sentences.zh: 奪謂易其常分者也。

translations.literal: Duo means changing one’s constant allotment.
translations.idiomatic: “Lose composure” means abandoning one’s usual measure.

sentences.zh: 注[五]事見何進傳。

translations.literal: Note [5] The affair appears in He Jin’s biography.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The confrontation is told in He Jin’s memoir.

sentences.zh: 杜預注左傳曰：「戕者，卒暴之名也。」

translations.literal: Du Yu’s commentary on the Zuo Tradition says: “Qiang means the name of a sudden violent act.”
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu glosses qiang as a violent, sudden end.

sentences.zh: 注[六]孔子曰：「君子無終食之閒違仁，造次必於是，顛沛必於是。

translations.literal: Note [6] Confucius said: “The gentleman does not for the space of a meal depart from benevolence—in haste it must be so, in ruin it must be so.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Confucius said the gentleman never drops humanity, in haste or in ruin.

sentences.zh: 」馬融注云：「造次，急遽也。

translations.literal: ” Ma Rong’s commentary says: “Zaoci means hurried haste.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong glosses zaoci as sudden hurry.

sentences.zh: 顛沛，僵仆也。

translations.literal: Dianpei means falling prostrate.
translations.idiomatic: Dianpei means falling flat.

sentences.zh: 雖急遽僵仆，不違仁也。」

translations.literal: Even in hurried haste and falling prostrate, he does not depart from benevolence.”
translations.idiomatic: Even then he does not abandon humanity.”

sentences.zh: 趙岐字邠卿，京兆長陵人也。

translations.literal: Zhao Qi, courtesy name Bingqing, was a man of Changling in the capital region.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi, courtesy name Bingqing, came from Changling in Jingzhao.

sentences.zh: 初名嘉，生於御史臺，因字臺卿，[一]後避難，故自改名字，示不忘本土也。

translations.literal: Originally his name was Jia; he was born in the Censor Terrace, hence his courtesy name Taiqing, Note [1] later, fleeing troubles, he therefore changed his own name and courtesy name to show he did not forget his native soil.
translations.idiomatic: Born in the imperial censor yamen as Zhao Jia, he took the courtesy name Taiqing; later, fleeing persecution, he changed his name to Zhao Qi to mark his roots.

sentences.zh: 岐少明經，有才蓺，娶扶風馬融兄女。

translations.literal: Qi in youth understood the classics and had talent and arts; he married the daughter of Ma Rong’s elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: He mastered the classics young and married Ma Rong’s niece.

sentences.zh: 融外戚豪家，岐常鄙之，不與融相見。

translations.literal: Rong was a powerful house of outer-court kin; Qi always despised him and would not meet Rong.
translations.idiomatic: He despised Ma Rong’s flashy clan and refused to visit him.

sentences.zh: [二]仕州郡，以廉直疾惡見憚。

translations.literal: Note [2] He served commandery and province, and by incorruptibility and straightness, hating evil, was feared.
translations.idiomatic: In office his honesty and hatred of corruption made others wary.

sentences.zh: 年三十餘，有重疾，臥蓐七年，[三]自慮奄忽，乃為遺令□兄子曰：「大丈夫生世，遯無箕山之操，[四]仕無伊、呂之勳，天不我與，復何言哉！

translations.literal: After thirty sui he had a grave illness and lay on the mat seven years; Note [3] expecting sudden death, he therefore made a testament to his elder brother’s son, saying: “A great man born in the world—if in reclusion he lacks the conduct of Mount Ji, in office the merit of Yi and Lü, Heaven does not grant me my span—what more is there to say!
translations.idiomatic: At thirty he fell mortally ill for seven years and drafted a will for his nephew: “If I lack Xu You’s reclusion or Yi Yin’s service, Heaven denies me my time—so be it!

sentences.zh: 可立一員石於吾墓前，刻之曰：『漢有逸人，姓趙名嘉。

translations.literal: You may set a round stone before my tomb and carve on it: “Han had a recluse, surname Zhao, given name Jia.
translations.idiomatic: Raise one round stone at my grave and carve: “Here lies Zhao Jia, recluse of Han.

sentences.zh: 有志無時，命也柰何！

translations.literal: He had ambition but not the times—fate—what can be done about it!”
translations.idiomatic: Ambition without a season—blame fate, not me!”

sentences.zh: 』」其後疾瘳。

translations.literal: ”” Afterward the illness healed.
translations.idiomatic: He recovered from the illness.

sentences.zh: 注[一]以其祖為御史，故生於臺也。

translations.literal: Note [1] Because his grandfather was a censor, he was therefore born in the terrace.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: His grandfather’s post explains a birth in the censor yamen.

sentences.zh: 注[二]三輔決錄注曰：「岐娶馬敦女宗姜為妻。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Sanfu jue lu commentary says: “Qi married Ma Dun’s daughter Zongjiang as wife.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Sanfu jue lu names his wife as Ma Dun’s daughter.

sentences.zh: 敦兄子融嘗至岐家，多從賓與從妹宴飲作樂，日夕乃出。

translations.literal: Dun’s nephew Rong once came to Qi’s house with many guests and held a drinking party with a younger female cousin until evening before leaving.
translations.idiomatic: Ma Rong once caroused at Zhao’s home with a crowd and a cousin until nightfall.

sentences.zh: 過問趙處士所在。

translations.literal: He passed by asking where Recluse Zhao was.
translations.idiomatic: On leaving he asked where “Recluse Zhao” might be.

sentences.zh: 岐亦厲節，不以妹拿之故屈志於融也。

translations.literal: Qi also kept strict integrity and did not bend his will to Rong because of his younger sister’s connection.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi kept his distance and would not bend to Ma Rong for a marriage tie.

sentences.zh: 與其友書曰：『馬季長雖有名當世，而不持士節，三輔高士未曾以衣裾襒其門也。

translations.literal: In a letter to his friend he said: “Though Ma Jichang has a name in the age, he does not maintain a gentleman’s integrity—no high gentleman of the three adjuncts has ever let his robe hem brush his gate.”
translations.idiomatic: He wrote a friend that Ma Rong had fame but no integrity—no worthy of the capital would darken his door.

sentences.zh: 』岐曾讀周官二義不通，一往造之，賤融如此也。」

translations.literal: ” Qi once read two passages of the Offices of Zhou he did not understand and went once to visit him—he despised Rong to this degree.”
translations.idiomatic: He once swallowed pride to ask Ma Rong about the Zhou li—then went back to scorning him.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]蓐，寢蓐也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Ru means the sleeping mat.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Ru is the sickbed mat.

sentences.zh: 聲類曰：「蓐，薦也。」

translations.literal: The Sheng lei says: “Ru means bedding.”
translations.idiomatic: The Sheng lei glosses ru as a pallet or mat.

sentences.zh: 注[四]易曰：「遯而亨，君子以遠小人。

translations.literal: Note [4] The Changes says: “Retreat and success—the gentleman thereby keeps far from petty men.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The hexagram Dun tells the gentleman to flee the mean.

sentences.zh: 」王弼註：「遯之義，避內而之外者也。

translations.literal: ” Wang Bi’s commentary: “The meaning of dun is to leave the inner and go to the outer.”
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bi reads dun as withdrawal from the inner to the outer world.

sentences.zh: 」箕山，許由所隱處也。

translations.literal: Mount Ji is where Xu You hid.
translations.idiomatic: Mount Ji is where Xu You hid from Yao’s offer.

sentences.zh: 永興二年，辟司空掾，議二千石得去官為親行服，朝廷從之。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongxing he was summoned as clerk to the Minister of Works, debated whether two-thousand-dan officials might leave office to observe mourning for parents, and the court followed it.
translations.idiomatic: In 154 he served the Minister of Works and won a rule letting governors resign to mourn parents.

sentences.zh: 其後為大將軍梁冀所辟，為陳損益求賢之策，冀不納。

translations.literal: Afterward he was summoned by Grand General Liang Ji and presented policies on gain and loss and seeking worthies; Ji did not adopt them.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji later summoned him for reform memos and ignored them.

sentences.zh: 舉理劇，為皮氏長。

translations.literal: Recommended for handling difficult cases, he became magistrate of Pishi.
translations.idiomatic: Nominated for “hard cases,” he became magistrate of Pishi.

sentences.zh: [一]會河東太守劉佑去郡，而中常侍左悺兄勝代之，岐恥疾宦官，即日西歸。

translations.literal: Note [1] It happened that Administrator of Hedong Liu You left the commandery, and the elder brother of palace attendant Zuo Guan, Sheng, replaced him; Qi, shamed and hating eunuchs, that very day returned west.
translations.idiomatic: When a eunuch’s brother replaced Liu You as governor of Hedong, Zhao Qi resigned on the spot and went home west.

sentences.zh: 京兆尹延篤復以為功曹。

translations.literal: Metropolitan Governor Yan Du again employed him as merit clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du of Jingzhao rehired him as merit clerk.

sentences.zh: 注[一]皮氏故城在今絳州龍門縣西。

translations.literal: Note [1] The old walled town of Pishi lies west of Longmen county in Jiangzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Han Pishi stood west of present Longmen in Shanxi.

sentences.zh: 決錄曰「岐為長，抑強討奸，大興學校」也。

translations.literal: The Jue lu says “As magistrate Qi suppressed the strong, punished the wicked, and greatly expanded schools.”
translations.idiomatic: The Jue lu praises his term for crushing magnates and building schools.

sentences.zh: 先是中常侍唐衡兄玹為京兆虎牙都尉，[一]郡人以玹進不由德，皆輕侮之。

translations.literal: Earlier the elder brother of palace attendant Tang Heng, Xuan, had been Colonel of Tiger Teeth in the capital region; Note [1] the people of the commandery, because Xuan had advanced not through virtue, all despised and insulted him.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier Tang Xuan, brother of the eunuch Tang Heng, held a capital military post; locals despised his unmerited rise.

sentences.zh: 岐及從兄襲又數為貶議，玹深毒恨。

translations.literal: Qi and his cousin Xi also repeatedly voiced belittling criticism; Xuan deeply hated them.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi and his cousin Zhao Xi mocked him until Tang Xuan burned with hate.

sentences.zh: [二]延熹元年，玹為京兆尹，岐懼禍及，乃與從子戩逃避之。

translations.literal: Note [2] In the first year of Yanxi, Xuan became Metropolitan Governor; Qi feared disaster would reach him, and therefore fled in hiding with his nephew Jian.
translations.idiomatic: In 158 Tang Xuan became Metropolitan Governor; Zhao Qi fled with his nephew Jian.

sentences.zh: 玹果收岐家屬宗親，陷以重法，盡殺之。

translations.literal: Xuan indeed seized Qi’s family members and kin, trapped them with heavy law, and killed them all.
translations.idiomatic: Tang Xuan arrested every Zhao kinsman and executed them on trumped-up charges.

sentences.zh: [三]岐遂逃難四方，江、淮、海、岱，靡所不歷。

translations.literal: Note [3] Qi then fled disaster in the four directions—Jiang, Huai, sea, and Dai—there was nowhere he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi wandered the Yangzi, Huai, coast, and Taishan—nowhere was too far.

sentences.zh: 自匿姓名，賣餅北海市中。

translations.literal: He hid his name and sold cakes in the market of Beihai.
translations.idiomatic: He hid his identity and peddled cakes in Beihai.

sentences.zh: 時安丘孫嵩年二十餘，游市見岐，察非常人，停車呼與共載。

translations.literal: At that time Sun Song of Anqiu was over twenty; wandering the market he saw Qi, perceived he was no ordinary man, stopped his cart and called him to ride together.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Song of Anqiu, twenty-something, spotted him in the market, sensed quality, and offered a lift.

sentences.zh: 岐懼失色，嵩乃下帷，令騎屏行人。

translations.literal: Qi feared and changed color; Song then lowered the curtain and ordered his riders to screen passersby.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi blanched; Sun Song drew the curtains and cleared the street.

sentences.zh: 密問岐曰：「視子非賣餅者，又相問而色動，不有重怨，即亡命乎？

translations.literal: He secretly asked Qi, saying: “Looking at you, you are no cake-seller; again, when questioned your color moved—do you not harbor a deep grudge, or are you a fugitive?
translations.idiomatic: He whispered, “You are no baker—your face betrays a blood feud or a fugitive’s fear.”

sentences.zh: 我北海孫賓石，闔門百口，埶能相濟。

translations.literal: I am Sun Bingshi of Beihai—with a household of a hundred mouths I have the means to aid you.”
translations.idiomatic: “I am Sun Bingshi of Beihai; my whole clan can hide you.”

sentences.zh: 」岐素聞嵩名，即以實告之，遂以俱歸。

translations.literal: ” Qi had long heard Song’s name and therefore told him the truth, and they then returned together.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi knew Sun Song’s reputation and told the truth; they rode home together.

sentences.zh: 嵩先入白母曰：「出行，乃得死友。

translations.literal: Song first entered and told his mother: “Going out on the road, I have gained a friend unto death.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Song told his mother, “I went out and found a friend for life and death.”

sentences.zh: 」迎入上堂，饗之極歡。

translations.literal: ” He welcomed him into the upper hall and feasted him with utmost joy.
translations.idiomatic: He feasted him in the main hall with highest joy.

sentences.zh: 藏岐復壁中數年，岐作□屯歌二十三章。

translations.literal: He hid Qi in a double wall for several years; Qi composed twenty-three stanzas of the □ Tun song.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Song hid him between walls for years while Zhao Qi wrote twenty-three stanzas of his “Ailment” ballad.

sentences.zh: 注[一]玹音玄。

translations.literal: Note [1] Xuan is read xuan.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The graph 玹 is read like “xuan.”

sentences.zh: 注[二]決錄註：「襲字符嗣。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Jue lu commentary: “Xi’s courtesy name was Zisi.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Zhao Xi, courtesy Zisi—

sentences.zh: 先是杜伯度、崔子玉以工草書稱於前代，襲與羅暉拙書，見蚩於張伯英。

translations.literal: Earlier Du Bodu and Cui Ziyu were famed in a former age for skilled cursive script; Xi with Luo Hui wrote clumsily and was mocked by Zhang Boying.
translations.idiomatic: —once ranked below Du and Cui as a calligrapher and was mocked by Zhang Zhi.

sentences.zh: 英頗自鄉高，與朱賜書云『上比崔、杜不足，下方羅、趙有餘』」也。

translations.literal: Ying was quite proud of himself; in a letter to Zhu Ci he said “above, compared to Cui and Du I fall short; below, compared to Luo and Zhao I have surplus.””
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhi boasted in a letter that he stood below the masters but above Luo and Zhao.”

sentences.zh: 注[三]決錄注曰：「岐長兄盤，州都官從事，早亡。

translations.literal: Note [3] The Jue lu commentary says: “Qi’s eldest brother Pan was a commandery clerk in the provincial bureau and died early.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: His elder brother Pan died young as a provincial clerk.

sentences.zh: 次兄無忌，字世卿，部河東從事，為玹所殺。

translations.literal: The next brother Wuji, courtesy name Shiqing, served as Hedong clerk and was killed by Xuan.”
translations.idiomatic: His brother Wuji, Hedong clerk, was murdered by Tang Xuan.”

sentences.zh: 」戩音翦。

translations.literal: ” Jian is read jian.
translations.idiomatic: The nephew’s name Jian is read jian.

sentences.zh: 後諸唐死滅，因赦乃出。

translations.literal: Later, when the various Tang houses died out and were extinguished, because of an amnesty he then came out.
translations.idiomatic: When the Tang clan fell and an amnesty was proclaimed, he emerged from hiding.

sentences.zh: 三府聞之，同時並辟。

translations.literal: The three high bureaus heard of it and at the same time all summoned him.
translations.idiomatic: All three ministries summoned him at once.

sentences.zh: 九年，乃應司徒胡廣之命。

translations.literal: Only in the ninth year did he answer Minister of Education Hu Guang’s summons.
translations.idiomatic: In the ninth year he accepted Hu Guang’s call.

sentences.zh: 會南匈奴、烏桓、鮮卑反叛，公卿舉岐，擢拜并州刺史。

translations.literal: It happened that the Southern Xiongnu, Wuhuan, and Xianbei rebelled; the dukes and ministers recommended Qi and promoted him to Governor of Bingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: When northern tribes revolted, the court made him Governor of Bingzhou.

sentences.zh: 岐欲奏守邊之策，未及上，會坐黨事免，因撰次以為禦寇論。

translations.literal: Qi wished to memorialize a policy for guarding the frontier; before he could submit it, he happened to be dismissed for partisan affairs, and therefore arranged his materials into a treatise “Repelling Raiders.”
translations.idiomatic: Partisan charges struck him down before he could submit border plans; he turned his drafts into a “Repelling Raiders” essay.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]決錄注曰：「是時綱維不攝，閹豎專權，岐擬前代連珠之書四十章上之，留中不出。」

translations.literal: Note [1] Note [1] The Jue lu commentary says: “At that time the net did not hold, eunuchs monopolized power; Qi modeled forty chapters of linked-gem writing from former ages and submitted them—they were detained in the palace and not issued.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: He sent forty chapters of “linked pearl” memorials against eunuch rule; the palace suppressed them.

sentences.zh: 靈帝初，復遭黨錮十餘歲。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Ling’s reign he again suffered partisan proscription for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Ling he endured another decade of partisan blacklisting.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，四方兵起，詔選故刺史、二千石有文武才用者，征岐拜議郎。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhongping troops rose everywhere; an edict selected former governors and two-thousand-dan officials with civil and military ability, summoned Qi, and appointed him Court Gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: In 184 the court recalled veteran governors with talent; Zhao Qi was named a Court Gentleman.

sentences.zh: 車騎將軍張溫西征關中，請補長史，別屯安定。

translations.literal: General of Chariots and Cavalry Zhang Wen campaigned west into Guanzhong and asked to have him fill the post of senior clerk, encamped separately at Anding.
