translations.idiomatic: Zhang Wen’s western expedition took Zhao Qi as chief clerk with a separate camp at Anding.

sentences.zh: 大將軍何進舉為敦煌太守，行至襄武，[一]岐與新除諸郡太守數人俱為賊邊章等所執。

translations.literal: Grand General He Jin recommended him as Administrator of Dunhuang; when he reached Xiangwu, Note [1] Qi together with several newly appointed commandery governors was seized by the bandits Bian Zhang and others.
translations.idiomatic: En route to Dunhuang at Xiangwu he was captured with other new governors by Bian Zhang’s rebels.

sentences.zh: 賊欲脅以為帥，岐詭辭得免，展轉還長安。

translations.literal: The bandits wished to force him to be their leader; Qi used deceitful words to escape, and by twists and turns returned to Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: He talked his way out of leading the rebels and limped back to Chang’an.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]縣名，屬隴西郡。

translations.literal: Note [2] Note [1] It is a county name under Longxi commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Note two cites note one: Xiangwu was a county in Longxi.

sentences.zh: 注[二]決錄注曰「岐還至陳倉，復遇亂兵，裸身得免，在草中十二日不食」也。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Jue lu commentary says “When Qi returned to Cangcao he again met mutinous troops, escaped naked, and lay in the grass twelve days without eating.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: A gloss adds that he later fled naked through mutiny at Cangcao and fasted twelve days in the weeds.

sentences.zh: 及獻帝西都，復拜議郎，稍遷太僕。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xian moved the capital west, he was again appointed Court Gentleman and gradually promoted to Grand Coachman.
translations.idiomatic: After the move west with Emperor Xian he rose again to Court Gentleman and then Grand Coachman.

sentences.zh: 及李傕專政，使太傅馬日磾撫慰天下，以岐為副。

translations.literal: When Li Jue monopolized government he sent Grand Tutor Ma Midi to pacify the realm, with Qi as deputy.
translations.idiomatic: Li Jue sent Ma Midi on a goodwill tour of the empire with Zhao Qi as second.

sentences.zh: 日磾行至洛陽，表別遣岐宣揚國命，所到郡縣，百姓皆喜曰：「今日乃復見使者車騎。」

translations.literal: When Midi reached Luoyang he memorialized to send Qi separately to proclaim the state’s commands; in every commandery and county he reached, the people joyfully said: “Today we see the envoy’s carriage again.”
translations.idiomatic: Ma Midi asked Zhao Qi to carry the imperial voice east; villagers rejoiced to see a Han envoy’s chariot again.

sentences.zh: 是時袁紹、曹操與公孫瓚爭冀州，紹及操聞岐至，皆自將兵數百里奉迎，岐深陳天子恩德，宜罷兵安人之道，又移書公孫瓚，為言利害。

translations.literal: At that time Yuan Shao, Cao Cao, and Gongsun Zan were struggling for Ji province; Shao and Cao, hearing Qi had arrived, each personally led troops several hundred li to welcome him; Qi set forth at length the Son of Heaven’s gracious virtue and the way to lay down arms and pacify the people, and also sent a letter to Gongsun Zan explaining gain and loss.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shao and Cao Cao rode hundreds of li to meet him; he preached the emperor’s mercy, urged disarmament, and wrote Gongsun Zan on the folly of civil war.

sentences.zh: 紹等各引兵去，皆與岐期會洛陽，奉迎車駕。

translations.literal: Shao and the others each withdrew their troops and all made an appointment with Qi to meet in Luoyang and welcome the imperial carriage.
translations.idiomatic: They pulled back their hosts and promised to rally in Luoyang for the emperor’s return.

sentences.zh: 岐南到陳留，得篤疾，經涉二年，期者遂不至。

translations.literal: Qi went south to Chenliu, contracted a severe illness, and for two years passed through it; those who had made appointments therefore did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: He fell desperately ill at Chenliu for two years, and the Luoyang rendezvous never happened.

sentences.zh: 興平元年，詔書征岐，會帝當還洛陽，先遣□將軍董承修理宮室。

translations.literal: In the first year of Xingping an edict summoned Qi; it happened the emperor was about to return to Luoyang and first sent General Dong Cheng to repair the palace.
translations.idiomatic: In 194 the court summoned him while General Dong Cheng was ordered to refurbish the Luoyang palaces for the emperor’s return.

sentences.zh: 岐謂承曰：「今海內分崩，唯有荊州境廣地勝，西通巴蜀，南當交址，年谷獨登，兵人差全。

translations.literal: Qi said to Cheng: “Within the seas there is only ruin; only Jing province is broad of territory and strong of terrain—it connects west to Ba-Shu and fronts south to Jiaozhi; its harvests alone are good and its soldiers and people comparatively intact.
translations.idiomatic: He told Dong Cheng that only Liu Biao’s Jingzhou still had grain, defensible ground, and links to Shu.

sentences.zh: 岐雖迫大命，猶志報國家，欲自乘牛車，南說劉表，可使其身自將兵來□朝廷，與將軍並心同力，共□王室。

translations.literal: Though Qi is pressed by the end of life, his will is still to repay the state; I wish to ride in an ox cart south and persuade Liu Biao—he can be made to lead troops in person to come to the court’s aid, and with the general of one mind and united strength together support the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: Though dying, he offered to ride an ox cart south to persuade Liu Biao to march to Luoyang and join Dong Cheng in upholding the throne.

sentences.zh: 此安上救人之策也。

translations.literal: This is the plan to settle the heights and save the people.”
translations.idiomatic: That was his plan to save the dynasty.”

sentences.zh: 」承即表遣岐使荊州，督租糧。

translations.literal: Cheng immediately memorialized dispatching Qi as envoy to Jingzhou to oversee rent grain.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Cheng memorialized the mission and put him in charge of shipping grain.

sentences.zh: 岐至，劉表即遣兵詣洛陽助修宮室，軍資委輸，前後不絕。

translations.literal: When Qi arrived, Liu Biao at once sent troops to Luoyang to help repair the palace; military supplies were forwarded in relays without cease.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Biao sent troops and endless supplies to rebuild Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 時孫嵩亦寓於表，表不為禮，岐乃稱嵩素行篤烈，因共上為青州刺史。

translations.literal: At that time Sun Song also lodged with Biao; Biao did not treat him courteously; Qi therefore praised Sun Song’s plain conduct as stern and constant, and together they memorialized him as Inspector of Qingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Biao snubbed Sun Song, Zhao Qi praised Sun’s integrity and won him the Qingzhou inspectorship.

sentences.zh: 岐以老病，遂留荊州。

translations.literal: Qi because of old age and illness therefore remained in Jingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Age and illness kept Zhao Qi in Jingzhou.

sentences.zh: 曹操時為司空，舉以自代。

translations.literal: Cao Cao at the time was Minister of Works and recommended him to replace himself.
translations.idiomatic: Cao Cao, then Minister of Works, nominated him as his successor.

sentences.zh: 光祿勳桓典、少府孔融上書薦之，於是就拜岐為太常。

translations.literal: Director of Retainers Huan Dian and Privy Treasurer Kong Rong submitted memorials recommending him; thereupon he was appointed Minister of Ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: Huan Dian and Kong Rong seconded the call, and he became Grand Master of Ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 年九十餘，建安六年卒。

translations.literal: At more than ninety sui he died in the sixth year of Jian’an.
translations.idiomatic: He died in 201, aged over ninety.

sentences.zh: 先自為壽藏，[一]圖季札、子產、晏嬰、叔向四像居賓位，又自畫其像居主位，皆為讚頌。

translations.literal: Earlier he had prepared his own longevity tomb, Note [1] with painted images of Jizha, Zichan, Yan Ying, and Shu Xiang in the guest seats, and also painted his own image in the host seat, all with eulogistic verses.
translations.idiomatic: He designed his own tomb with portraits of four ancient worthies as “guests” and himself as host, each with a verse.

sentences.zh: □其子曰：「我死之日，墓中聚沙為默，布簟白衣，散發其上，覆以單被，即日便下，下訖便掩。

translations.literal: He ordered his son: “On the day I die, heap sand in the tomb for silence, spread a mat and white clothes, loosen my hair upon them, cover with a single quilt—bury the same day when finished; when finished, then close.”
translations.idiomatic: He instructed his son: simple sand floor, white robe, loose hair, one cover—same-day burial, then seal the vault.”

sentences.zh: 」岐多所述作，蓋孟子章句、三輔決錄傳於時。

translations.literal: Qi had many writings; in general the Mengzi chapter glosses and Sanfu jue lu circulated in the age.
translations.idiomatic: His Mengzi commentary and Records of the Three Adjuncts long circulated among scholars.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]壽藏謂塚壙也。

translations.literal: Note [1] A “longevity crypt” means the tomb pit.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: “Longevity crypt” means the grave chamber prepared in life.

sentences.zh: 稱壽者，取其久遠之意也。

translations.literal: Calling it “longevity” takes the sense of long endurance.
translations.idiomatic: The word “longevity” wishes long endurance for the site.

sentences.zh: 猶如壽宮、壽器之類。

translations.literal: It is like “longevity palace” or “longevity vessels.”
translations.idiomatic: The same usage appears in other “longevity” tomb terms.

sentences.zh: 頤在今荊州古郢城中也。

translations.literal: Yi is in the old Ying city of Jingzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: His tomb lay in the old Ying capital in Hubei.

sentences.zh: 注[二]決錄序曰：「三輔者，本雍州之地，世世徙公卿吏二千石及高貲，皆以陪諸陵。

translations.literal: Note [2] The preface to the Jue lu says: “The three adjuncts were originally Yongzhou land; generation after generation moved dukes, ministers, two-thousand-dan clerks, and the very rich, all to attend the imperial tombs.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Sanfu jue lu preface describes the forced settlement of elite families around the Han tombs.

sentences.zh: 五方之俗雜會，非一國之風，不但繫於詩秦、豳也。

translations.literal: The customs of the five directions mixed together—it is not the air of a single state, not only tied to the “Qin” and “Bin” odes in the Poetry.
translations.idiomatic: The region blended customs from every quarter, not merely the Qin and Bin airs of the Classic of Poetry.

sentences.zh: 其為士好高尚義，貴於名行。

translations.literal: Its gentlemen esteem high conduct and value name and deed.
translations.idiomatic: Local gentlemen prized lofty principle and reputation.

sentences.zh: 其俗失則趣埶進權，唯利是視。

translations.literal: When its customs fail, they rush to power and advance through influence, seeing only profit.
translations.idiomatic: When morals failed, men chased connections and profit.

sentences.zh: 余以不才，生於西土，耳能聽而聞故老之言，目能視*[而]*見衣冠之疇，心能識而觀其賢愚。

translations.literal: I, lacking talent, was born in the western lands; my ears can hear and I listen to the words of old men, my eyes can see *[and]* behold the ranks of caps and gowns, my mind can know and observe their worthy and foolish.
translations.idiomatic: The author says he grew up hearing elders and watching the capital’s elite.

sentences.zh: 常以玄冬，夢黃發之士，姓玄名明，字子真，與余寤言，言必有中，善否之閒，無所依違，命操筆者書之。

translations.literal: Always in deep winter I dreamed of a hoary-headed gentleman surnamed Xuan, given name Ming, courtesy name Zizhen, who spoke with me awake—his words always hit the mark; between good and ill he never wavered, and I ordered the brush-holder to write them down.
translations.idiomatic: He records dreams of a sage named Xuan Ming dictating true judgments he set down in winter.

sentences.zh: 近從建武以來，暨於斯今，其人既亡，行乃可書，玉石朱紫，由此定矣，故謂之決錄矣。」

translations.literal: From recent Jianwu down to the present, those men are gone but their deeds can be written; jade and stone, cinnabar and purple are thereby fixed—therefore it is called a “decisive record.””
translations.idiomatic: From Guangwu to his own day he fixed reputations in writing—hence the title Jue lu (“Decisive Record”).”

sentences.zh: 贊曰：吳翁溫愛，義干剛烈。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Wu the elder was warm in love; with duty he confronted fierce integrity.
translations.idiomatic: The verse praises Wu You’s gentle speech and brave remonstrance.

sentences.zh: [一]延、史字人，風和恩結。

translations.literal: Note [1] Yan and Shi treated people with harmony and tied them with kindness.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Du and Shi Bi won men by kindness.

sentences.zh: 梁使顯刑，誣黨潛絕。

translations.literal: Liang’s envoy brought conspicuous punishment; the falsely accused faction was secretly cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Bi’s defiance of Liang Ji’s agents broke the partisan round-up.

sentences.zh: 子幹兼姿，逢掖臨師。

translations.literal: Zigan combined bearing and learning; in wide sleeves he faced the teacher.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhi (“Zigan”) brought both dignity and learning to Ma Rong’s lecture hall.

sentences.zh: [二]邠卿出疆，專出朝威。

translations.literal: Note [2] Bingqing crossed the border; alone he displayed the court’s authority.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi crossed the line of personal safety to speak for the Han in Dong Zhuo’s hall.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]謂以義干梁冀爭李固也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Note [1] It means with duty he remonstrated with Liang Ji over Li Gu.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The first gloss still refers to Wu You’s protest to Liang Ji over Li Gu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]禮記孔子曰：「丘少居魯，衣逢掖之衣。

translations.literal: Note [2] The Record of Rites: Confucius said: “In youth I dwelt in Lu and wore the fengye robe.”
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The Liji cites Confucius on the wide-sleeved robe of Lu scholars.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注曰：「逢猶大也。

translations.literal: ” Zheng Xuan’s commentary says: “Feng means large.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses feng as “large.”

sentences.zh: 為大掖之衣，此君子有道蓺者所衣也。

translations.literal: It is the robe with large sleeves that gentlemen with the Way and arts wear.”
translations.idiomatic: It was the gown of cultivated gentlemen.”

sentences.zh: 」相承本作縫，義亦通。

translations.literal: ” The transmitted text writes feng as “sew”—the meaning also fits.
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts write a homophone for “sew”; both readings work.

sentences.zh: 注[三]疆，界也。

translations.literal: Note [3] Jiang means boundary.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: Jiang means a border or limit.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「大夫出疆，苟利社稷，專之可也。」

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: “When a great officer goes beyond his border, if it profits the altars of soil and grain, he may act on his own authority.”
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition authorizes ministers abroad to act for the altars’ good.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Editorial collation (notes on textual variants) follows.

sentences.zh: 二０九九頁七行常牧豕於長垣澤中按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁紀作「長羅澤」。

translations.literal: Page 2099 line 7: “often herded pigs in the Changyuan marsh”—according to: the Jijie cites Hui Dong saying the Yuan ji writes “Changluo marsh.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Hui Dong notes the Yuan ji reads “Changluo marsh” instead of “Changyuan marsh.”

sentences.zh: 水經注雲圈稱言長垣縣有羅亭，故長羅縣也，後漢並長垣。

translations.literal: The Shui jing zhu says Qu Cheng states that Changyuan county had a Luo pavilion, hence former Changluo county; Later Han merged it into Changyuan.
translations.idiomatic: The Shui jing zhu identifies the place with the old Changluo county seat.

sentences.zh: 有長羅澤，季英牧豕處。

translations.literal: There is a Changluo marsh, where Jiying herded pigs.
translations.idiomatic: The marsh where Wu You kept pigs is thus identified as Changluo.

sentences.zh: 二０九九頁一二行裴氏廣*

translations.literal: Page 2099 line 12: Pei’s Guang*
translations.idiomatic: Collation note on Pei’s Guang—

translations.literal: (chuan)
translations.idiomatic: (chuan)

sentences.zh: **[州]*記據殿本考證改。

translations.literal: **[zhou]* ji—emended per the Dian ben textual investigation.
translations.idiomatic: —emended to Guangzhou ji per the palace edition’s collation.

sentences.zh: 二一００頁一行桂陽甲騎九真都龐按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂水經注「甲騎」作「騎田」，「都龐」作「部龍」。

translations.literal: Page 2100 line 1: “Guiyang armored cavalry; Jiuzhen Dulang”—according to: the Jijie cites Shen Qinhan saying the Shui jing zhu writes “Qitian” for “armored cavalry” and “Bulong” for “Dulang.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Shen Qinhan cites the Shui jing zhu’s variant place names.

sentences.zh: 又按：汲本「都龐」作「都寵」。

translations.literal: Also according to: the Ji ben writes “Dulong” for “Dulang.”
translations.idiomatic: The Ji manuscript writes “Dulong.”

sentences.zh: 二一００頁一四行及*

translations.literal: Page 2100 line 14: and*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: “and”—

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si)

sentences.zh: **[犯]*軷據殿本改。

translations.literal: **[fan]* the ba—emended per the Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: —“cross the ba”—emended per the palace edition.

sentences.zh: 按：殿本考證謂「犯」字監本誤「祀」，據周禮大馭文改正。

translations.literal: According to: the Dian ben textual investigation states the Jian ben mistakenly wrote “si” for “fan”; corrected from the Zhou li Grand Charioteer text.
translations.idiomatic: The error si for fan is corrected from the Zhou li.

sentences.zh: 二一００頁一五行*[犯]*軷*

translations.literal: Page 2100 line 15: *[fan]* the ba*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: fragment on “crossing the ba”—

translations.literal: (si)
translations.idiomatic: (si)

sentences.zh: *者據殿本改。

translations.literal: *—emended per the Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: —emended per the palace edition.

sentences.zh: 二一００頁一五行以*[菩]*芻棘□為神主據刊誤補，與周禮鄭注合。

translations.literal: Page 2100 line 15: “with *[pu]* straw and thorns □ as the spirit host”—supplemented per the Kanwu, matching Zheng Xuan’s Zhou li commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restores straw/thorn wording to match Zheng Xuan.

sentences.zh: 二一０一頁六行觀過斯知人矣按：殿本「人」作「仁」，疑後人據論語改。

translations.literal: Page 2101 line 6: “see his faults and you know the man”—according to: the Dian ben writes “ren” as “benevolence”; suspect later editors altered it from the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian ben’s “benevolence” for “man” may be a late change from the Analects.

sentences.zh: 錢大昕謂古書仁人二字多通用，然以「人」義為長。

translations.literal: Qian Daxin says benevolence and man interchange in old texts, yet the sense “man” is preferable.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin argues both graphs interchange but “man” fits better.

sentences.zh: 二一０一頁七行安丘男子毋丘長按：「毋」原斗「母」，逕據殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2101 line 7: “man of Anqiu Wuqiu Chang”—according to: “Wu” was originally miswritten “mother”; emended straight from the Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: corrects 母 to 毋 for the surname Wuqiu.

sentences.zh: 二一０一頁一０行明府雖加哀鄉汲本、殿本「鄉」作「矜」。

translations.literal: Page 2101 line 10: “though my lord adds pity xiang”—Ji ben and Dian ben write “jin” (pity) for “xiang.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Dian read “pity” instead of “xiang.”

sentences.zh: 按：段注說文作「鄉」，雲□矛令聲。

translations.literal: According to: Duan’s Shuowen commentary writes “xiang,” explaining □ mao phonetic.
translations.idiomatic: Duan Yucun’s Shuowen note keeps “xiang.”

sentences.zh: 二一０二頁一行年二十二按：殿本作「年三十二」。

translations.literal: Page 2102 line 1: “at twenty-two”—the Dian ben reads “at thirty-two.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian ben gives thirty-two instead of twenty-two.

sentences.zh: 二一０三頁一行延篤字叔堅按：集解引汪文臺說，謂御覽四百五十二引謝承書，雲「字叔固」。

translations.literal: Page 2103 line 1: “Yan Du courtesy name Shujian”—according to: the Jijie cites Wang Wentai saying the Yu lan 452 quotes Xie Cheng’s book with “courtesy name Shugu.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Xie Cheng may give Shugu instead of Shujian.

sentences.zh: 二一０三頁一行旬日能諷之按：殿本「諷」下有「誦」字。

translations.literal: Page 2103 line 1: “within ten days could recite it”—the Dian ben adds the word “chant” after “recite.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian ben adds “chant” after “recite.”

sentences.zh: 二一０三頁七行嗟乎延生按：「乎」原作「呼」，逕據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: Page 2103 line 7: “Ah, Master Yan”—according to: “hu” was originally written “call”; emended straight from Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restores 乎 for a miswritten 呼.

sentences.zh: 二一０三頁一一行南平陽故城*[在]*今兗州鄒縣據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2103 line 11: “old town of Nanpingyang *[lies]* in Zou county of Yanzhou today”—supplemented per Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: supplies missing “lies” per Ji and Dian.

sentences.zh: 二一０五頁一行其為仁之本與按：集解引錢大昕說，謂葛本「仁」作「人」，今本論語作「仁」，初學記友悌部、御覽人事部引論語俱作「人」，與有子先言「其為人也孝弟」，後言「其為人之本」，首尾相應，亦當以「人」為長也。

translations.literal: Page 2105 line 1: “the root of benevolence”—according to: the Jijie cites Qian Daxin saying the Ge ben writes “man” for “benevolence”; today’s Analects reads “benevolence”; Chuxue ji and Yu lan citations of the Analects both write “man,” matching Youzi’s first saying “his conduct is filial” and later “the root of his conduct”—“man” is also preferable.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Qian Daxin argues the older Analects text read “man” not “benevolence.”

sentences.zh: 二一０六頁一０行坐於客堂按：集解引沉欽韓說，謂「客」一本作「容」，是也。

translations.literal: Page 2106 line 10: “sat in the guest hall”—according to: the Jijie cites Shen Qinhan saying one manuscript writes “rong” (screen), which is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: “guest” may be “screened hall” (rong).

sentences.zh: 隱蔽自障者皆謂之容。

translations.literal: Those who conceal themselves behind screens are all called rong.
translations.idiomatic: Commentators gloss rong as screened privacy.

sentences.zh: 堂前有屏蔽之設，故曰容堂。

translations.literal: Where there is screening before the hall, it is therefore called a rong hall.
translations.idiomatic: Hence “hall of screens.”

sentences.zh: 二一０八頁一三行悉條諸生聚斂奸吏按：殿本考證謂「生」字疑衍。

translations.literal: Page 2108 line 13: “drew up a full list of licentious students and venal clerks”—according to: the Dian ben textual investigation suspects the word “students” is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian suspects a superfluous “students.”

sentences.zh: 二一０九頁一行桓帝弟渤海王悝何焯校本改「渤」為「勃」。

translations.literal: Page 2109 line 1: “Emperor Huan’s younger brother Prince Huai of Bohai”—He Zhuo’s collated edition changes the first character of the commandery name (渤) to the alternate form 勃 for consistency.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: He Zhuo normalizes the spelling of Bohai to match the rest of the chapter.

sentences.zh: 按：下文皆作「勃」，故何氏改為一律。

translations.literal: According to: the text below all uses the 勃 spelling, so He made them uniform.
translations.idiomatic: The following text uses the 勃 form consistently.

sentences.zh: 二一一０頁三行伍被勸淮南*

translations.literal: Page 2110 line 3: Wu Bei urged the Prince of Huainan*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Huainan—

translations.literal: (zi)
translations.idiomatic: (zi)

sentences.zh: **[王]*謀反誅也據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[wang]* to rebel—emended per Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: —heir—emended per Ji and Dian.

sentences.zh: 二一一一頁五行*

translations.literal: Page 2111 line 5:*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: marginal note—

translations.literal: (feng)
translations.idiomatic: (feng)

sentences.zh: **[俸]*音扶用反據汲本、殿本改，與正文合。

translations.literal: **[feng]* read fuyong fan—emended per Ji ben and Dian ben to match the main text.
translations.idiomatic: —salary—emended to match the received text.

sentences.zh: 二一一一頁八行生乃說以它事謁弼按：刊誤謂案文「說」字當作「詭」，謂詭譎也。

translations.literal: Page 2111 line 8: “the student then pleaded another matter to visit Bi”—according to: the Kanwu says examining the text the word “plead” should be “gui” (deceive), meaning deceitful.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Kanwu argues shui should read gui (“deceive”).

sentences.zh: 二一一一頁一０行侯覽大怨按：殿本「怨」作「怒」。

translations.literal: Page 2111 line 10: “Hou Lan nursed a great grudge”—the Dian ben writes “anger” for “grudge.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian uses “anger” instead of “grudge.”

sentences.zh: 二一一二頁八行洪字子林按：殿本「林」作「休」。

translations.literal: Page 2112 line 8: “Hong’s courtesy name was Zilin”—the Dian ben writes “Xiu” for “Lin.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian gives Zixiu instead of Zilin.

sentences.zh: 二一一二頁一四行似夫儒者汲本、殿本「儒」作「懦」。

translations.literal: Page 2112 line 14: “like a Ru scholar”—Ji ben and Dian ben write “ruo” (timid) for “Ru.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Dian read “timid” rather than “Ru.”

sentences.zh: 按：說文儒，柔也。

translations.literal: According to: the Shuowen defines Ru as “soft.”
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen glosses ru as “soft.”

sentences.zh: 儒有懦弱義，非斗字。

translations.literal: Ru carries the sense of timidity—it is not a miswritten character.
translations.idiomatic: The sense “soft/timid” is intentional, not a scribal error.

sentences.zh: 二一一三頁三行聞活千人者有封*[子]*孫據殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2113 line 3: “heard that he who saves a thousand men will have enfeoffed *[descendants]*”—supplemented per the Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Dian supplies “descendants” in the quotation.

sentences.zh: 二一一三頁三行*[後]*世其興乎據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2113 line 3: “*[later]* generations will rise?”—supplemented per Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Dian supply “later” in the quotation.

sentences.zh: 二一一六頁二行發起□謬按：集解引惠棟說，謂「□謬」疑「紕繆」之訛。

translations.literal: Page 2116 line 2: “exposed □ errors”—according to: the Jijie cites Hui Dong saying “□ error” is probably a corruption of “pimi” (twisted error).
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Hui Dong emends a garbled phrase to pimi.

sentences.zh: 二一一六頁三行無力供繕*[寫]*上據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 2116 line 3: “lacked strength to supply a fair copy *[to present]* upward”—supplemented per Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Dian supply “to present” in Lu Zhi’s memorial.

sentences.zh: 二一一七頁一二行攘服災咎汲本、殿本「攘」作「禳」。

translations.literal: Page 2117 line 12: “rang for calamity and fault”—Ji ben and Dian ben write the graph for ritual “warding off” calamity (禳) instead of 攘.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Dian read the exorcistic character 禳 rather than 攘.

sentences.zh: 按：攘禳通。

translations.literal: According to: the graphs for “seize” and “exorcise” interchange here.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary: The characters for “seize” and “ritual expulsion” are used interchangeably in this note.

sentences.zh: 二一一八頁一行朓者月行速在日前按：「日」原斗「目」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2118 line 1: “tiao means the moon runs fast before the sun”—according to: “sun” was originally miswritten “eye”; emended straight from Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: corrects a miswritten “eye” to “sun” in the gloss on tiao.

sentences.zh: 二一一八頁一行君舒緩則臣*

translations.literal: Page 2118 line 1: when the ruler is lax then ministers*
translations.idiomatic: Collation: continuation of Liu Xiang’s line—when the ruler slackens, ministers—

translations.literal: (jiao)
translations.idiomatic: The marginal gloss marks an alternate character for the word rendered “arrogant” in the line above.

sentences.zh: **[驕]*慢據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: **[jiao]* grow arrogant—emended per Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: —“arrogant”—emended per Ji and Dian editions.

sentences.zh: 二一一八頁七行後以王甫程阿所構按：「甫」原斗「封」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 2118 line 7: “later, through slander by Wang Fu and Cheng A”—according to: “Fu” was originally miswritten “seal”; emended straight from Ji ben and Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restores Wang Fu’s name from a miswritten “seal.”

sentences.zh: 二一一九頁六行今先害*[之]*刊誤謂案文少「之」字，不成文理。

translations.literal: Page 2119 line 6: “now if you first harm *[him]*”—the Kanwu says examining the text it lacks the word “him,” which breaks the sense.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Kanwu notes the object “him” is missing in the quotation.

sentences.zh: 又集解引惠棟說，謂先賢傳云「今先害之」。

translations.literal: Also the Jijie cites Hui Dong saying the Xianxian zhuan reads “now if you first harm him.”
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong cites a fuller text of the Xianxian zhuan.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplemented accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The edition supplies “him” accordingly.

sentences.zh: 二一二一頁五行趙岐字邠卿按：此傳「岐」字原本皆作「歧」，汲本同。

translations.literal: Page 2121 line 5: “Zhao Qi courtesy name Bingqing”—according to: in this biography the character “Qi” was originally written “歧” throughout, same in Ji ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: early editions wrote the homophone “歧” for Zhao Qi’s name.

sentences.zh: 王先謙謂殿本「歧」作「岐」，古書通作，以「岐」為是。

translations.literal: Wang Xianqian says the Dian ben writes “岐” for “歧”; old books use both, but “岐” is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Xianqian prefers the standard graph 岐 used in the palace edition.

sentences.zh: 今一律依殿本改為「岐」。

translations.literal: Now uniformly emended to “岐” following the Dian ben.
translations.idiomatic: The text is normalized to 岐 per the Dian ben.

sentences.zh: 二一二一頁七行年三十餘有重疾按：御覽五百一引「三十餘」作「四十餘」。

translations.literal: Page 2121 line 7: “after thirty sui he had a grave illness”—according to: the Yu lan 501 quotes “after forty sui.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yu lan gives forty rather than thirty sui.

sentences.zh: 二一二五頁一行目能視*[而]*見衣冠之疇據汲本補。

translations.literal: Page 2125 line 1: “my eyes can see *[and]* behold the ranks of caps and gowns”—supplemented per Ji ben.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji ben supplies the conjunction “and” in Zhao Qi’s preface.

sentences.zh: 二一二五頁一行常以玄冬夢黃發之士集解引惠棟說，謂據御覽三百九十九卷引「玄冬」下有「修夜思而未之得也忽然而寢」十二字，「夢」下有「此」字。

translations.literal: Page 2125 line 1: “always in deep winter dreamed of a hoary-headed gentleman”—the Jijie cites Hui Dong saying that according to Yu lan juan 399 after “deep winter” twelve characters follow: “long night thinking yet not grasping it, suddenly falling asleep,” and after “dream” the word “this.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Hui Dong cites a longer Yu lan quotation with extra lines before the dream.

sentences.zh: 今按：御覽「士」作「叟」。

translations.literal: Now according to: the Yu lan writes “old man” for “gentleman.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Yu lan reads “old man” instead of “gentleman.”

sentences.zh: 二一二五頁一行字子真按：惠棟謂御覽引「字」下有「曰」字。

translations.literal: Page 2125 line 1: “courtesy name Zizhen”—according to: Hui Dong says the Yu lan quotation after “courtesy name” has the word “said.”
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Hui Dong notes Yu lan inserts “said” after the courtesy name.
